The talent hero occupies roughly 50–60% of the initial viewport height on desktop, with ample negative space above and below the main identity text. The banner and utility cards beneath it remain relatively shallow, preserving a calm rhythm of horizontal bands. In the main content area, the left column gets about two-thirds of the width for imagery and reviews, while the right column takes one-third for compact yet airy cards. Section breaks between major blocks (hero to banner, banner to main content, gallery to reviews) use 48–64px vertical spacing, while elements within a card or section rely on 16–24px internal padding.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density is low and refined, echoing an editorial magazine look. Each component sits in ample whitespace with clear breathing room on all sides. Text elements are few and well-sized; there is no clutter of small UI controls in the hero. Interactive panels like booking and messaging are arranged in short vertical flows with clear separation between fields and actions. Lists such as reviews are spaced so that each item reads like a discrete editorial block, with comfortable 16–24px gaps between items. Overall, the page favors fewer, larger elements over many small ones.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts horizontally at the site header (logo and navigation) and then drops into a strong vertical focus on the talent hero: first the portrait background and large serif name in the center, then the secondary hero strip with social metrics near the bottom of the hero. From there, the eye descends to the shortlist-redirect-banner and into the main two-column area: leftward to the large portfolio gallery, and rightward to the availability and booking stack. The right column forms a vertical utility spine where availability leads naturally into date/shortlist association, then booking and messaging, and finally the subscription upsell. Modal overlays temporarily interrupt this flow by centering the user’s attention in the middle of the screen, while sticky behavior on the booking block keeps the primary action in view as the left column scrolls.

layout.overall_description: Below the fixed global header, the talent profile opens with a full-bleed hero band where a blurred grayscale portrait collage fills the background edge to edge. Centered within this field of soft texture, the talent’s name appears in large serif type, spanning one to two lines, with a restrained line of supporting details beneath. Ample space above and below this text keeps the hero calm and uncluttered. Near the lower portion of the hero, a translucent horizontal strip carries the social metrics, floating lightly over the imagery. As the hero ends, a slim, full-width banner sits on a white background, signaling any shortlist context without breaking the minimal aesthetic. Below, the content pulls into a centered, max-width container where the page splits into a balanced two-column layout: on the left, a generously sized portfolio gallery dominates, followed by well-spaced reviews and a subtle link to similar talent; on the right, a vertical stack of airy cards handles availability, shortlist date association, booking (with a subtle budget indicator), messaging, and a softly highlighted subscription upsell. The right-hand booking card can remain in view as the user scrolls, while the left column’s imagery and reviews flow past, preserving a premium, editorial feel. When needed, full-screen overlays for loading and shortlist selection gently dim the page and place a minimal, centered panel in front, maintaining the same calm, high-end tone.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the data-loader appears as a full-viewport overlay above all page content, including the global header. It uses a semi-opaque white or very light gray wash that covers the entire screen from top edge to bottom, slightly muting the underlying layout while still hinting at its structure. At the exact center of the viewport, a minimal loading indicator (spinner or progress glyph) and a small line of status text are vertically and horizontally centered, with ample empty space surrounding them. The overlay has no visible chrome beyond this central cluster, in keeping with the clean, editorial DNA. When inactive, this component is completely hidden and does not occupy space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position, full-screen overlay that sits on the topmost layer; it scrolls with the viewport but typically disables scrolling while visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The talent main info panel acts as the hero band and sits immediately below the global header, spanning the full width of the viewport. On desktop, it occupies roughly half to 60% of the viewport height with a minimum height of about 320–400px, creating a broad, cinematic strip. A grayscale, slightly blurred portrait image or collage fills this band edge to edge as the background. The content is arranged in a single centered column: the talent’s name is centered both horizontally and vertically within an inner max-width (e.g., 960–1200px) that is aligned to the page center. The name is set in large serif type and may break onto two lines; beneath it, with about 16–24px vertical spacing, a smaller sans-serif line lists role (e.g., “Fashion / Commercial”), location, and possibly age or agency tag. There is at least 64px of negative space above the name and 64px below the supporting line before reaching the edges of the hero, preserving an airy, editorial feel. On smaller screens, the hero height adjusts to remain comfortably visible (around 50% of viewport height) and the centered alignment is maintained.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section flowing with the page; it remains at the top of the scrollable content and scales responsively in height based on viewport size.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The portfolio gallery sits in the left column of the main content area, directly beneath the hero and any shortlist banner. The overall content container is centered with a max-width (around 1200px), and the left column takes roughly two-thirds of that width (about 8 out of 12 grid columns). The gallery spans the full width of this left column, starting with a section title aligned to the left and a generous 24px margin below the title before the first image. The gallery itself forms a tall visual block, typically 400–600px in visible height on desktop before scrolling within the page continues. Images are arranged in a clean grid or full-width carousel, with consistent 8–16px gaps between thumbnails or frames, and ample white margin (24–32px) separating the gallery from adjacent sections above (hero/banners) and below (reviews). The gallery reads as the primary visual content below the hero, drawing the eye naturally downward from the talent’s name.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; it participates in the regular page scroll and flexes in height based on the number of media items and responsive layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The social metrics panel is embedded within the lower portion of the talent hero, overlaying the background image while remaining visually subordinate to the name. It appears as a slim horizontal bar, centered horizontally within the same max-width as the hero text and positioned near the bottom third of the hero area, leaving a comfortable 32–48px gap between it and the bottom edge of the hero. The bar spans approximately 40–60% of the hero width, with softly rounded corners and a semi-transparent white or dark overlay behind the metrics for legibility. Inside, metrics such as follower counts and engagement rates are arranged in a single row with even spacing, using compact sans-serif labels. There is generous internal padding (about 12–16px vertically and 24px horizontally), keeping the bar light and unobtrusive. On mobile, this bar remains centered but may expand closer to full width to accommodate the content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned within the hero section and scrolls away with it; no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the main content area’s two-column grid, the availability calendar occupies the top of the right column, which is about one-third of the overall content width (4 of 12 grid columns). Its top aligns roughly with the top edge of the portfolio gallery to its left, though a slight 16–24px vertical offset is acceptable to maintain comfortable spacing below any shortlist-redirect-banner. The calendar appears as a clean card with a white background, subtle border or shadow, and 16–24px of padding on all sides. A concise title sits at the top left of the card, with the month selector and navigation aligned to the right if present, leaving about 12–16px of space before the calendar grid. The calendar grid fills most of the card’s height, which is roughly 280–360px on desktop. There is ample white space around date cells, and the bottom of the card sits about 24px above the shortlist-date-association-bar that follows. On smaller screens, this card will span the full width, stacking above booking and other right-column content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column’s flow; on desktop it scrolls normally above the sticky booking block, and on mobile it loses any relationship to stickiness and becomes part of a straightforward vertical stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shortlist date association bar is a narrow, horizontally oriented form directly beneath the availability calendar within the right column. It spans the full width of that column and has a relatively shallow height of about 56–64px, presenting as a band with a subtle background distinction (light gray or a very soft tint) to separate it from the white calendar and booking card. A short label or prompt is left-aligned, vertically centered within the bar, while the primary control (e.g., dropdown or button to select/add a shortlist) is placed to the right within the same bar, with at least 16px padding from both the left and right edges. The bar maintains generous horizontal padding (16–24px) but avoids additional vertical clutter, preserving the clean, editorial feel. There is a 24–32px gap between this bar and the booking panel below, allowing each card-like element to remain visually distinct.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline component in the right column; it scrolls with the page and sits directly between the calendar and booking panel without sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites list selector appears only when invoked and overlays the entire viewport. A semi-opaque dark backdrop covers the screen from edge to edge, muting the page content beneath while keeping its structure faintly visible. Centered both vertically and horizontally is a refined, medium-sized modal panel (approximately 480–560px wide on desktop, scaling down on smaller devices) with a white background and ample internal padding of 24–32px. The modal’s title is left-aligned at the top with a close icon in the top right corner, separated from the main form content by a 16–24px gap. The body of the modal contains the list selector controls arranged in a vertical stack with clear 16–24px spacing between fields and options. Primary and secondary actions are placed in a horizontal row at the bottom right, with about 24px of whitespace below them before the edge of the panel. The overall effect is minimal and high-end, matching the rest of the site.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position, centered modal overlay with a full-screen dimmed backdrop; it sits above all other content and scroll is typically locked while it is open.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking panel resides in the right column of the main content area, directly beneath the shortlist-date-association-bar with a vertical gap of about 32px. It spans the full width of this one-third column and appears as a prominent card with a white background, subtle shadow or hairline border, and 24px padding on all sides. At the top, a compact header row displays the booking title left-aligned, with the booking-budget-indicator nestled at the right edge of the same row. A 16–24px gap separates this header from the main booking form elements, which are stacked vertically: date or range confirmation, location and usage fields, followed by any notes or options, each separated by 16–24px of vertical space. At the bottom, primary actions (e.g., “Request Booking”) are right-aligned, with sufficient space below to avoid visual crowding. This panel is the densest functional area on the page but maintains the overall airy spacing to stay aligned with the editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, the booking panel is a sticky card within the right column: as the user scrolls the page, it remains fixed relative to the viewport once its top reaches about 96px below the global header, staying in view while the left column (portfolio and reviews) continues to scroll. It stops sticking when reaching the bottom of its container. On tablet and mobile widths, the sticky behavior is disabled and the panel becomes a normal, full-width block in the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking budget indicator is visually integrated into the booking panel rather than occupying its own block. It appears as a small pill-shaped badge or compact label aligned to the right side of the booking panel’s header row, level with the booking title text. The badge is about the height of a single line of text plus 8–12px of vertical padding, with 12–16px of horizontal padding, and may use the teal brand color for its border or background to subtly draw attention. The surrounding whitespace in the header ensures the badge does not feel cramped: there is at least 16px padding between the badge and the right edge of the panel, and 8–12px of space between the badge and the booking title on the left. The indicator updates dynamically but does not add additional vertical bulk to the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored within the top header row of the booking panel; it moves along with the booking panel and inherits any sticky behavior the panel has on larger screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The message actions panel is placed immediately below the booking panel in the right column, separated by a vertical gap of about 32px to maintain visual hierarchy. It is a smaller, simpler card that spans the full width of the right column, with the same white background and subtle border treatment for consistency. Internal padding is slightly lighter (around 16–24px) to keep the card compact. A concise headline such as “Contact Talent” sits at the top left, followed by one or two primary actions (e.g., message buttons, templates) arranged either in a single horizontal row or a short vertical stack, each spaced by 12–16px. The lower edge of the card is left with 16–24px of padding, ensuring the actions don’t crowd the border. The card’s overall height is relatively shallow compared to the booking panel, reinforcing its role as a secondary, yet important, action area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; it scrolls normally beneath the sticky booking panel and sits above the subscription upsell panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reviews section is positioned in the left column beneath the portfolio gallery. After a generous 48–64px vertical gap following the bottom edge of the gallery, a left-aligned section title introduces the reviews. The content spans the full width of the left column, presenting each review as an individual block in a vertical list. Each review block has comfortable internal spacing—16–24px padding above and below the review text or metadata—and there is 24–32px of vertical space between successive reviews, allowing each to read like a separate editorial piece. The section as a whole maintains a broad band of white space on top and bottom, aligning with the magazine-like feel, and extends down the page as needed. At the bottom of the reviews, there is additional spacing (48px) before reaching the similar-talent-link, giving a clear sense of section closure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content in the left column; it scrolls continuously beneath the portfolio gallery as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The similar talent link appears as the final element in the left column, following the reviews section. It is separated from the last review by about 48px of vertical space to signal a transition from content to navigation. The link itself is left-aligned and spans only as wide as its text, perhaps accompanied by a subtle arrow icon. It sits within a relatively open area of white space, with 16–24px of padding above and below before any page footer, giving it a light, understated presence. The typography is consistent with other navigation links—simple sans-serif with a teal accent on hover—ensuring it reads clearly as an exit path to related profiles without competing visually with primary content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element at the bottom of the left column; it scrolls with the rest of the content and does not have any sticky or overlay behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shortlist redirect banner sits directly beneath the hero (talent-main-info-panel) and above the main two-column content area. It spans the full width of the page’s content container: on wide screens, this means it is centered with the same max-width as the main content (around 1200px), with white margins on the sides if the viewport is wider. The banner is shallow—approximately 56–72px in height—and appears as a horizontal bar with a subtle background tint or a thin teal accent line or block on the left edge. The text inside is left-aligned, vertically centered within the bar, and there may be a secondary action (e.g., “View shortlist”) aligned to the right. Internal horizontal padding of 24px on both sides keeps the content from touching the edges. There is a clear 32–48px vertical gap between the bottom of this banner and the beginning of the portfolio/gallery and right column content, maintaining the spacious, editorial rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section between hero and main content; it scrolls away normally and does not stick to the top once passed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The subscription upsell panel is the last card in the right column, appearing beneath the message-actions-panel with a vertical gap of about 48px. It spans the full width of the right column and is presented as a slightly more visually distinct card, possibly using a very light teal-tinted background to differentiate it from the functional booking and messaging panels. Internal padding is generous—24–32px on all sides—to give the content a relaxed, promotional feel. The copy is center- or left-aligned, with a brief headline, one short explanatory sentence, and a single primary call-to-action button, each separated by 12–16px. Surrounding white space above and below the panel reinforces that this is optional and non-intrusive, preserving the premium feel of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; it appears only when relevant (conditional) and scrolls normally with the rest of the column content.

name: Favorites & Lists
title: Favorites & Lists
description: Manage talent shortlists and custom favorites lists, organize talents for campaigns, and verify that each list meets specific composition requirements.

core_features: Load all available talent lists for the current user and set an active list, optionally based on the active_list_id URL parameter (using getTalentListsSummary).
Display the talents in the active list, including key information such as name, gender, age, location, hourly rate, rating, and social reach (using getTalentListItems).
Create new favorites lists with a name, type, and optional description (using createTalentList).
Rename existing talent lists (using renameTalentList).
Delete existing talent lists with confirmation and automatic fallback to another active list (using deleteTalentList).
Add a talent to the active list by specifying a talent identifier and optional selected date and notes (using addTalentToList).
Remove a talent from the active list (using removeTalentFromList).
Move a talent from one list to another (using moveTalentBetweenLists).
Open a full talent profile in a separate page when the user clicks a talent card, passing the talentId via URL to talent_profile.html.
Navigate to talent_search.html with parameters based on the active list’s dominant location and primary category when the user clicks "Find more like this".
Define and run composition checks against the active list (e.g., combinations of gender, age ranges, and location) using client-side evaluation against the loaded list items.
user_workflows:
View and switch between lists:
1. User opens favorites.html, optionally with ?active_list_id in the URL.
2. Page calls getTalentListsSummary.
3. If active_list_id matches a returned TalentList.id, that list is set as active; otherwise, the default shortlist (is_default or list_type=shortlist) is selected, or the first list if no default is flagged.
4. Page calls getTalentListItems with the active list id.
5. The active list’s items are displayed; the user can click another list in the list-switcher to change the active list.
6. When the active list changes, the page updates the URL active_list_id parameter and reloads items via getTalentListItems for the newly selected list.

Create a new favorites list:
1. User clicks a "Create list" action in the list-switcher.
2. A modal form opens where the user enters a list name, chooses a list_type (e.g., favorites_list), and optionally adds a description.
3. On submit, the page calls createTalentList with the entered values.
4. On success, the new TalentList is appended to the list-switcher, the URL active_list_id is updated to the new list’s id, and getTalentListItems is called for that id (which will return an empty items array initially).

Rename an existing list:
1. User selects a list in the list-switcher and clicks a rename action.
2. A rename modal opens with the current name prefilled.
3. User updates the name and submits.
4. Page calls renameTalentList with listId and new_name.
5. On success, the updated list name is reflected in the list-switcher and any list header areas without reloading the page; the active_list_id stays unchanged.

Delete a list:
1. User selects a list and clicks a delete action.
2. A confirmation dialog displays the list name and warns that its list items will no longer be accessible.
3. If the user confirms, the page calls deleteTalentList with the listId.
4. On success, the list is removed from the list-switcher.
5. If the deleted list was active, the page selects a new active list: first a list where is_default is true or list_type=shortlist if available, otherwise another remaining list; the URL active_list_id is updated accordingly and getTalentListItems is called for the new active list.
6. If no lists remain, the page displays an empty state and hides list-dependent controls.

Add a talent to a list from this page:
1. With an active list selected, the user opens an "Add talent" control (e.g., button that opens a modal or inline form).
2. User enters a talent identifier (and optionally a selected date and notes).
3. On submit, the page calls addTalentToList with the active list id, the talentId, and any provided selected_date and notes.
4. On success, the new list item id is returned; the page refreshes the active list by calling getTalentListItems again or by appending the new item to the existing in-memory items collection.
5. The user now sees the new talent in the active list.

Remove a talent from a list:
1. In the active list view, each talent row or card includes a remove action.
2. User clicks remove for a specific talent.
3. The page calls removeTalentFromList with the active list id and the talent’s id.
4. On success, the removed item is either filtered out from the in-memory items array or the page calls getTalentListItems again to refresh the list.
5. The list count and any metrics dependent on the items update automatically.

Move a talent between lists:
1. In the active list view, user clicks a move action for a specific talent.
2. A modal opens showing a dropdown of other available lists from getTalentListsSummary (excluding the current list).
3. User selects a destination list and confirms.
4. The page calls moveTalentBetweenLists with fromListId, toListId, and talentId.
5. On success, the talent is removed from the current list’s items and, depending on UX, either immediately added to the in-memory items of the destination list (if already loaded) or loaded later when that destination list is activated via getTalentListItems.

Open a talent profile from a list:
1. User clicks on a talent card or name in the list.
2. The page navigates to talent_profile.html, appending ?id={talentId} taken from the talent object in the list item.
3. No API call is made on this page; the target page handles loading the full profile based on the id parameter.

Find more similar talent based on a list:
1. With an active list, user clicks a "Find more like this" action in the list header.
2. The page computes listDominantLocation and listDominantCategory on the client side by analyzing the currently loaded items from getTalentListItems (e.g., choosing the most frequent location_display and primary_category among talents, ignoring items without these properties).
3. The page navigates to talent_search.html with ?location={listDominantLocation}&primary_category={listDominantCategory} in the URL.
4. The search page uses these parameters to show similar talent.

Verify composition against campaign requirements:
1. With an active list, user opens a composition checker panel.
2. User defines requirements via inputs (e.g., required total count, counts per gender, allowed age range, required location city or state) and submits the check.
3. The component uses the already loaded list items from getTalentListItems, reading each talent’s gender, age, and location_display to evaluate the rules client-side.
4. The component calculates:
   - How many talents in the list match each rule.
   - Whether each rule is satisfied or missing.
5. Results are displayed as a pass/fail status per rule and an overall status for the active list (no additional API calls are made).
interactions: When the page initializes, it reads the active_list_id URL parameter, calls getTalentListsSummary, determines the active list, then calls getTalentListItems for that list.
Clicking on a list name in the list-switcher updates the active list, changes the active_list_id URL parameter, and triggers getTalentListItems for the newly selected list.
Clicking the "Create list" button opens a form modal and, on submit, calls createTalentList; success updates the list collection and activates the new list.
Clicking a "Rename" action on a list opens a rename modal and, on submit, calls renameTalentList; success updates the list’s name where displayed.
Clicking a "Delete" action on a list opens a confirmation dialog; on confirm, deleteTalentList is called and, upon success, the deleted list is removed and an alternative active list is selected.

Clicking an "Add talent" action opens an input form for talentId, selected_date, and notes; submitting the form calls addTalentToList for the active list and refreshes or augments the talent items view.
Clicking a "Remove" action on a talent within the active list calls removeTalentFromList with the active list id and the talent’s id; success updates the list items view.
Clicking a "Move" action on a talent opens a modal listing all other available lists; submitting the form calls moveTalentBetweenLists and updates the current list’s items.
Clicking on a talent name or card navigates to talent_profile.html?id={talentId}.
Clicking "Find more like this" in the list header calculates dominant attributes from current items and navigates to talent_search.html with location and primary_category as URL parameters.

Changing any field in the composition checker form updates the internal composition rule state; clicking a "Run check" button evaluates the current list items against these rules and updates the visible pass/fail results without additional API calls.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state for this page is the currently active talent list and its items. The active list is synchronized with the URL via the active_list_id query parameter and is not stored in localStorage.

Initial load:
- On page load, the script reads the active_list_id parameter from the current URL (if present).
- The page calls getTalentListsSummary to retrieve all TalentList objects for the user.
- If active_list_id matches a TalentList.id in the response, that list is set as the active list.
- If active_list_id is missing or does not match any list, the page selects a fallback active list in this order:
  1) A list where is_default is true, if any.
  2) A list where list_type is "shortlist", if any.
  3) Otherwise, the first list in the returned array.
- Once an active list is determined, the script calls getTalentListItems with that list’s id and stores both the list metadata and items in memory for the session.

Active list changes:
- When the user selects a different list from the list-switcher, the active list id in the internal state is updated.
- The URL is updated to include or replace the active_list_id query parameter with the new list id (e.g., favorites.html?active_list_id={id}). This makes the state shareable as a deep link.
- After changing the active list, the page calls getTalentListItems for the new id and replaces the items in memory; the composition checker and list header recompute any derived values based on the new items.

List mutation logic:
- After createTalentList succeeds, the new list is appended to the in-memory list collection, active_list_id in the URL is set to the new id, and getTalentListItems is called for the new list (expected to be empty initially).
- After renameTalentList succeeds, the in-memory representation of the list is updated with the new name; if the renamed list is active, list headers and the list-switcher reflect the updated name without changing URL parameters.
- After deleteTalentList succeeds, the deleted list is removed from the in-memory lists collection. If it was active, the page computes a new active list using the same fallback order as the initial load and updates the URL active_list_id parameter; then it calls getTalentListItems for the new active list (if any lists remain). If no lists remain, the URL may omit active_list_id and the UI shows an empty state.

Item mutation logic:
- After addTalentToList succeeds, the page can either:
  - Re-call getTalentListItems for the active list id and replace the items array, or
  - Append a newly constructed item object (based on the returned list_item_id, if enough information is available from other contexts). To avoid assumptions, a full refresh via getTalentListItems is recommended.
- After removeTalentFromList or moveTalentBetweenLists succeeds, the in-memory items array for the affected list is updated by filtering out the removed talent. If the destination list for a move is currently active, the destination list’s items are refreshed by calling getTalentListItems; otherwise, they are refreshed only when that list becomes active.

Derived state:
- The list header and "Find more like this" logic derive listDominantLocation and listDominantCategory from the active list’s loaded items. This derived state is recalculated every time getTalentListItems returns a new items array for the active list.
- The composition checker stores its own input rules in component-level state (e.g., required counts per gender, age minimum/maximum, required location). When the rules change or when the active list’s items change, the composition results are recalculated in memory without additional API calls.

Navigation state:
- Navigating away via talent cards or "Find more like this" uses URL parameters only. For talent_profile.html, the id parameter is taken directly from each item’s talent.id. For talent_search.html, location and primary_category are taken from the derived dominant values; the current page does not persist any extra state in localStorage.

components.functionality: On page load, fetches all talent lists, determines the active list based on the active_list_id URL parameter and fallback rules, and loads the items for the active list.
Displays all available TalentList entries, highlights the active list, and provides actions to switch lists, create new lists, rename existing lists, and delete lists.
Handles creating and renaming lists. For create, collects name, list_type, and optional description and calls createTalentList. For rename, collects a new name and calls renameTalentList.
Asks the user to confirm deletion of a list and, on confirmation, calls deleteTalentList. On success, notifies the list-loader to recompute the active list and refresh state.
Shows the active list’s name, type, description, and item count. Provides actions such as "Find more like this" and a toggle to show/hide the composition checker.

Displays the active list’s items with key talent summary data (name, gender, age, location_display, hourly_rate, rating_overall, social_reach_total) and item metadata (added_at, selected_date, notes). Provides actions to open the talent profile, remove the talent from the list, and initiate moving the talent to another list.
Allows the user to add a talent to the active list by specifying a talent identifier and optional selected_date and notes, then calls addTalentToList and refreshes the active list items.
Allows the user to move a talent from the active list to another list. Presents a selectable list of destination TalentList entries and, on submit, calls moveTalentBetweenLists.

Lets the user define composition rules (such as counts per gender, age range, and location criteria) and evaluates whether the active list’s talents satisfy these rules using the loaded TalentListItem and TalentProfile summaries. Shows pass/fail status per rule and overall.

Keeps the active list state and URL query parameter active_list_id in sync. Updates the URL when the active list changes and informs other components when the URL changes (e.g., via browser navigation).

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a full-width container for the hero and a fixed max-width inner container for header content and navigation links.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom hierarchy: global site header (logo + navigation) fixed at the top, followed immediately by a full-bleed hero for the Favorites & Lists page, then the working area for list management. Within the working area, use a simple two-column structure: the main column on the left for the talent list and list-level controls, and a narrower supporting column on the right for the composition checker. All controls and content follow a clear vertical stacking order: page hero → list selection → list actions → list contents and inline forms → analytical side panel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom hierarchy: global site header (logo + navigation) fixed at the top, followed immediately by a full-bleed hero for the Favorites & Lists page, then the working area for list management. Within the working area, use a simple two-column structure: the main column on the left for the talent list and list-level controls, and a narrower supporting column on the right for the composition checker. All controls and content follow a clear vertical stacking order: page hero → list selection → list actions → list contents and inline forms → analytical side panel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Keep the global header shallow (about 10–15% of viewport height) and allocate a visually dominant hero band beneath it (roughly 35–45% of the initial viewport height on desktop) with a full-bleed grayscale background image and centered typography. Below the hero, the main working area fills the rest of the viewport and scrolls. Horizontally, the content area uses a centered max-width container (e.g., 1140–1280px) inside the full-width background. Within that container, the left main column occupies about 65–70% of the width for the talent list, and the right column about 30–35% for the composition checker. Vertical spacing between functional blocks (hero → controls → list) uses 48–64px gaps; padding inside individual blocks and cards uses 16–24px.
layout_strategies.content_density: Reflect the calm, editorial feel: low density in the hero (headline and list selector only, no clutter), moderate density in the control toolbar (few, well-spaced actions), and slightly higher but still airy density in the talent list (cards or rows with comfortable 16–24px padding and ample line spacing). Avoid tight clusters of controls; navigation elements, buttons, and inline forms are spaced along an 8px grid with plenty of white space around them. The right-hand composition checker reads like a structured sidebar article—clear sections, generous margins, and no heavy visual noise.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the mockup’s horizontal-then-vertical flow. The user’s eye first hits the teal-accented logo and global navigation at the top, then drops into the full-bleed hero where a large, centered page title and current list context sit over a blurred grayscale image. From there, attention moves downward to the centrally aligned list switcher, then to the list header actions directly below. The vertical flow continues into the talent list on the left, with the right-hand composition checker forming a stable secondary focal area that is visible while scrolling. Teal accents and bold headings guide focus through the path: page title → current list selection → primary actions → individual talents → composition validation status.

layout.overall_description: Below the fixed global header, the Favorites & Lists page opens with a full-bleed grayscale background image stretching edge to edge, creating a hero band that fills a substantial portion of the first viewport. In the center of this hero, a large, elegant serif headline introduces the Favorites & Lists area, with a subtler line indicating the currently active list. Just beneath this typographic core, still visually connected to the hero, a horizontally centered list switcher allows users to move between lists without breaking the editorial calm. As the hero fades into a clean white background, a wide content container appears, slightly inset from the edges, where a slim, well-spaced toolbar for list-level actions runs across the top. Below this toolbar, the layout divides into a main left column and a narrower right column: on the left, an airy, magazine-like arrangement of talent cards or rows occupies most of the width, with an inline add-talent form anchored at the top; on the right, a vertically oriented composition checker panel reads like a refined sidebar article, maintaining a steady reference for list composition as users scroll the talents. When modals appear, they float centered over a dimmed version of this layout, maintaining the brand’s minimalist, high-end aesthetic with ample white space and restrained teal accents.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The list-loader is non-visual in its default state, initializing data before the visible components render. During loading states, it drives a subtle, full-width placeholder that sits in the main content area beneath the hero: a light-gray skeleton bar where the list switcher normally appears, a faint rectangular band where the list header actions toolbar would be, and a column of evenly spaced placeholder cards in the left main column where the talent list view will load. These skeleton elements respect the same max-width container, left/right column proportions, and generous vertical spacing (24–32px between skeleton rows) as the final layout, so the page structure feels stable even before data arrives.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual infrastructure component that controls a temporary loading state overlay within the main content container; it does not occupy persistent space once data is loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The list-switcher is positioned near the bottom of the hero band, horizontally centered within the full-width layout but constrained by the inner max-width container so it aligns with the content below. It sits directly beneath the large, centered hero headline, with about 24–32px of vertical space separating them, visually reading as a refined, single-row capsule of controls against the blurred grayscale background. The switcher spans roughly 60–70% of the container width, with its individual list options spaced evenly across. Each option has generous horizontal padding (16–24px) and minimal vertical thickness to maintain a sleek, tab-like feel, and the currently active list is accented with the teal brand color. On scroll, as the hero partially moves out of view, the list-switcher feels like the last element of the hero, leading the eye down into the list header actions below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored within the hero’s lower central region; it scrolls away with the hero and does not remain sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The list-management-modal appears centered both vertically and horizontally within the viewport, floating above the dimmed hero and main content. It occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop (capped around 560–640px) and maintains a tall but not full-height presence, with 32px padding on all sides and at least 48px of breathing room between its edges and the viewport borders. Inside, the modal content is laid out in a clean, single column: a bold, centered or left-aligned serif title at the top, form fields stacked with 16–24px vertical gaps, and primary/secondary buttons right-aligned at the bottom with sufficient space (16px) between them. The surrounding overlay is a semi-transparent black or deep gray at low opacity, muting the background while keeping the hero and list layout faintly visible, reinforcing the editorial, high-end feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as a centered, fixed-position overlay above all other content with a full-screen dimming layer; scroll is locked in the background while it is open.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The delete-list-confirmation dialog is a compact, focused overlay that appears centered in the viewport, slightly smaller than the list-management-modal to signal its simpler, single-decision nature. It occupies roughly 30–35% of the viewport width (around 420–480px on desktop) with 24–32px padding. The message is set in clear, well-spaced text, with the critical list name subtly emphasized. Buttons (e.g., Cancel and Delete) are aligned to the right at the bottom, with the destructive action in teal or a carefully chosen accent against a ghosted or outline style to remain refined. The dialog sits on the same dimmed overlay used by other modals, with ample negative space around the text to avoid clutter and to communicate the gravity of the action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position modal centered in the viewport with a full-screen dimming layer; it overlays all other components and blocks interaction until dismissed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The list-header-actions toolbar is positioned immediately below the hero region, aligned with the inner max-width content container and spanning nearly its full width. There is a generous vertical gap (48–64px) between the bottom edge of the hero/list switcher and the top of this toolbar, giving it room to breathe. Within the toolbar, actions are arranged horizontally in a single row: on the left, the current list name in a refined, slightly larger type serves as a contextual label; to the right, key buttons (e.g., New List, Rename, Share) sit with clear separation (at least 16px between buttons) and balanced padding (8px vertical, 16–24px horizontal). The entire toolbar has a low-profile height and may use a very subtle bottom rule or shadow to visually separate it from the list content below, but overall it stays minimal to match the editorial style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal band pinned at the top of the main content area; it scrolls out of view with the content and is not sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The talent-list-view occupies the left main column beneath the list-header-actions toolbar, starting just below it with a comfortable 32–48px vertical gap. It spans approximately 65–70% of the inner container width, beginning at the left margin and extending to a clear internal gutter (24–32px) before the right-hand composition checker panel. Vertically, it extends down the page and becomes the primary scrolling area. Inside this column, talents are displayed either as cards or rows, aligned in a single column to maintain a calm, editorial rhythm: each item uses a white background, subtle shadow or border, and 16–24px internal padding, with 16–24px vertical spacing between items. Imagery (model thumbnails) are grayscale or desaturated to match the site aesthetic, with teal accents reserved for key status indicators or selection states. The typography is clean with generous line-height, and there’s ample negative space at the left and right of each item to avoid visual clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls vertically as the main content area; its width is fixed relative to the max-width container, and it adjusts responsively to narrower screens (eventually stacking above the composition checker panel).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-talent-to-list-form is embedded at the top of the talent-list-view’s left column, just below the list-header-actions and above the first talent item. It aligns flush with the left column’s content and respects the same width (65–70% of container on desktop). The form is a low-profile horizontal strip with a clean, single-line input field and one or two compact buttons (e.g., Add, Search) arranged to the right of the input or immediately beneath it on smaller widths. The input is generously sized—spanning roughly 60–70% of the column width—with 8px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding. Vertical spacing above and below this inline form (24–32px) visually separates it from both the toolbar above and the talent items below, keeping the layout airy despite the functionality.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element anchored at the top of the talent-list-view column; it scrolls naturally with the list content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The move-talent-modal appears as a centered overlay similar in size to the list-management-modal but slightly more compact, focusing on selection rather than extensive input. It sits in the middle of the viewport, around 40–45% of the viewport width (approx. 520–560px), with 24–32px internal padding and at least 48px of space from the viewport edges. The layout inside is vertically stacked: a clear, concise heading at the top; a short explanatory line; then a list or dropdown of available target lists, each option spaced with at least 12–16px between rows to maintain clarity. The confirmation and cancel buttons are aligned right at the bottom, following the same spacing and typography styles as other modals. The dimmed background ensures focus stays on the modal while preserving a faint sense of the underlying hero and list layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position, centered overlay atop a full-screen dimming layer; it blocks interaction with the underlying page while open and scrolls independently if content exceeds its height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The composition-checker-panel is anchored in the right-hand column of the main content area, starting at the same vertical level as the top of the talent-list-view (just below the list-header-actions toolbar). It occupies approximately 30–35% of the container width on desktop, with a clear vertical separation (24–32px gutter) from the talent list. The panel runs vertically along the right side, styled like a refined sidebar: a clear, bold heading at the top, followed by stacked sections (e.g., gender balance, age ranges, diversity metrics) with ample spacing (16–24px) between them. Each metric line is concise with teal accents indicating status or thresholds, and generous padding (16–24px) within the panel ensures the content remains readable and uncluttered. The panel’s height extends down the page; as the user scrolls through a longer talent list, the top of the panel stays within view for as long as possible to keep composition feedback accessible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Right-column element that is sticky within the viewport on desktop: it remains fixed to the top of the viewport once the user scrolls past its initial position, until the bottom of the main content container is reached. On smaller screens, it stacks below the talent list and scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-sync component is invisible, handling routing and state persistence behind the scenes. Its presence is reflected indirectly through the layout: when users switch lists via the list-switcher or modify list-related state, the URL updates while the visible structures—hero, list header actions, talent list, and composition panel—remain in place with only their content swapped. There is no dedicated visual footprint for this component; instead, it ensures smooth transitions without page reloads, preserving scroll position within the talent list and the relative arrangement of all visible elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual infrastructure tied to the routing layer; it does not occupy spatial regions on the page and only influences how other components update and persist state during navigation.

name: Create Casting Call
title: Create Casting Call
description: Single-page workflow where clients or casting directors configure and submit a new casting call, including project details, talent requirements, compensation, visibility, and deadlines, then see inline confirmation with shortcuts to find and invite talent.

core_features: Load dynamic form options (talent roles, genders, rate types, visibilities, creator roles, currencies) using getCastingFormOptions and bind them to selection fields.
Pre-populate key casting fields (location_display, talent_category, gender) from incoming URL parameters when available.
Allow the user to select their creator role (e.g., client or casting director) before entering or submitting casting details, and bind this to creator_role.
Provide structured input sections for casting basics (title, location_display, talent_category, talent_role, gender, num_talent_needed, project_date, description).
Provide inputs for compensation (rate_type, rate_amount, currency) using options from getCastingFormOptions.
Provide inputs for visibility and timeline (visibility, application_deadline) using options from getCastingFormOptions.
Submit casting data via createCastingCall with status determined by the user action (e.g., draft vs published) and display inline success or error states.

After a successful publish, show a confirmation panel with contextual actions, including navigation to talent_search.html pre-filtered for this casting, and navigation to messages.html with subject and body_prefill derived from the created casting.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Open page and initialize form: 1) User navigates via header 'Post a Casting' or a direct CTA. 2) Page reads incoming URL parameters (location_display, talent_category, gender). 3) Page calls getCastingFormOptions. 4) Once options are loaded, the creator role selector and form fields become enabled. 5) If URL parameters are present and valid against the loaded options, the corresponding fields are pre-filled.

Workflow 2 – Select role and complete casting details: 1) User chooses their creator role in the role selector (mapped to creator_role). 2) User fills in or adjusts project title, location_display, talent_category, talent_role, gender, num_talent_needed, project_date, and description using the provided fields and select options. 3) User sets compensation by selecting rate_type and currency from the loaded options and entering rate_amount. 4) User chooses visibility from the loaded visibilities and, if desired, sets an application_deadline. 5) Form-level validation runs on field changes and before submission, preventing submission until required fields are valid according to system rules.

Workflow 3 – Submit casting as draft or publish: 1) User clicks one of the submission actions (e.g., Save as Draft or Publish Casting). 2) The submission handler compiles all current form values into the parameters required by createCastingCall, setting status according to the chosen action and creator_role from the role selector. 3) The page calls createCastingCall with these parameters and shows a submitting state (disabling actions). 4) On success, the page stores the returned casting_call object in page state, shows an inline confirmation panel, and resets any submission loading state. 5) On error, an error message is shown and the form remains visible for correction.

Workflow 4 – Use confirmation actions to find or invite talent: 1) After a successful publish, the confirmation panel displays key casting summary fields and contextual actions. 2) When the user clicks 'Find talent for this casting', the page navigates to talent_search.html with URL parameters location, primary_category, and gender derived from the submitted casting_call (location_display, talent_category, gender). 3) When the user clicks 'Invite shortlisted talent via message', the page navigates to messages.html with subject populated from casting_call.title and body_prefill composed dynamically from casting_call data and any available context, without relying on hardcoded sample text in this specification.
interactions: On page load, a data-initialization interaction reads URL parameters (location_display, talent_category, gender) and then calls getCastingFormOptions; form sections remain disabled until options are available.
When the user changes their creator role in the role selector, the bound creator_role state updates immediately and is later passed into createCastingCall on submission.

When the user edits any field in the casting form (title, location_display, talent_category, talent_role, gender, num_talent_needed, rate_type, rate_amount, currency, project_date, description, visibility, application_deadline), the corresponding in-memory casting draft state is updated and lightweight validation feedback may be displayed.

When the user clicks the primary publish action, a submission handler validates the current state and calls createCastingCall with status set to a value representing a published casting; while awaiting a response, the form prevents duplicate submissions.

When the user clicks a secondary save-as-draft action, the submission handler calls createCastingCall with status set to a value representing a draft casting; on success, a confirmation indicates that the draft has been created but may not be publicly visible.

If createCastingCall returns an error, an error interaction displays a non-hardcoded error message (supplied by the backend or generated from response metadata) and re-enables the submission actions so the user can correct and resubmit.

Upon successful createCastingCall, the confirmation panel interaction displays a summary using the returned casting_call object and surfaces two CTA buttons: one that constructs a URL to talent_search.html with query parameters based on casting_call fields and one that constructs a URL to messages.html with subject and body_prefill computed from casting_call fields.

When the user clicks 'Find talent for this casting', the interaction performs a client-side navigation to talent_search.html with URL parameters location, primary_category, and gender set from the submitted casting_call values; no additional APIs are called from this page.

When the user clicks 'Invite shortlisted talent via message', the interaction performs a client-side navigation to messages.html with URL parameters subject and body_prefill derived from casting_call.title and a dynamically generated message body.

If the user modifies pre-filled values (for example, changes gender or location_display that came from URL parameters), the updated values overwrite the initial defaults and are the ones used in createCastingCall and in all subsequent navigation URLs.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is initialized from URL parameters and API responses, without using localStorage. On load, the page reads the incoming URL parameters location_display, talent_category, and gender. These values are stored in a temporary initialization state and used to pre-fill the corresponding form fields once getCastingFormOptions resolves and provides compatible options. The primary page state consists of a draft casting object that mirrors the fields required by createCastingCall, along with auxiliary UI state flags such as loadingOptions, optionsError, isSubmitting, submitError, and submissionResult. The call to getCastingFormOptions populates enumerated option lists (talent_roles, genders, rate_types, visibilities, creator_roles, currencies) that are referenced throughout the form; until this call completes successfully, select inputs either remain disabled or show a loading state, and submission actions are blocked. When the user submits the form via a publish or draft action, the current draft casting state is validated and passed into createCastingCall, with status derived from the clicked action and creator_role from the role selector. The response from createCastingCall is stored as submissionResult.casting_call and submissionResult.message; presence of submissionResult.casting_call drives the rendering of the inline confirmation panel while the form remains visible for review or subsequent edits (if allowed by the implementation). For navigation to talent_search.html and messages.html, the page does not store any additional state outside the current session; instead, it derives outgoing URL parameters directly from the latest submissionResult.casting_call at the moment each CTA is clicked, ensuring that the navigated pages receive the most current casting data via their own URL parameters.

components.functionality: On initial page load, this component reads URL parameters (location_display, talent_category, gender), calls getCastingFormOptions to fetch dynamic form options, and prepares the initial draft casting state and option lists for all other components.

Displays a selector for the user to choose their creator role, sourcing available roles from getCastingFormOptions.creator_roles and binding the selected value to the draft casting's creator_role field.

Manages basic casting details including title, location_display, talent_category, talent_role, gender, num_talent_needed, project_date, and description. It uses the option lists from getCastingFormOptions for talent_role and gender and applies incoming URL parameter defaults where available.

Handles all compensation-related inputs for the casting, including rate_type (selected from getCastingFormOptions.rate_types), rate_amount, and currency (selected from getCastingFormOptions.currencies), and ensures values are prepared for submission via createCastingCall.

Provides controls to select visibility (using getCastingFormOptions.visibilities) and optionally set an application_deadline, binding both values to the draft casting so they can be passed to createCastingCall.

Coordinates validation and submission of the entire casting form. Based on the action the user triggers (publish or save draft), it sets the appropriate status value, aggregates all bound CastingCall fields (including creator_role, location_display, talent_category, talent_role, gender, num_talent_needed, rate_type, rate_amount, currency, project_date, description, visibility, application_deadline), and calls createCastingCall. It manages submission loading and error states and exposes the resulting casting_call to downstream components.

Renders the actionable buttons for submitting the casting (for example, publishing or saving as draft). It listens to click events and forwards the appropriate intent to casting-submission-controller while reflecting submission states (disabled/loading) driven by that controller.

Displays inline feedback about the result of calling createCastingCall, showing success or error messages and, on success, a brief summary of the created casting using fields from the returned casting_call object.

When a casting has been successfully created, this panel presents key read-only details from casting_call (such as title, location_display, talent_category, gender, visibility, project_date) and exposes contextual actions including 'Find talent for this casting' and 'Invite shortlisted talent via message', using the created casting as the source of navigation parameters.

Constructs and triggers navigation to talent_search.html after a successful casting creation. It reads the latest casting_call from submission state and generates a URL with parameters location, primary_category, and gender mapped respectively from casting_call.location_display, casting_call.talent_category, and casting_call.gender.

Constructs and triggers navigation to messages.html from the confirmation context. It uses the created casting_call to set the subject parameter (from casting_call.title) and composes a body_prefill parameter based on casting_call fields, so the messaging page can open with a prefilled invitation referencing the casting.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a full-width container for the hero and a fixed max-width inner container for header content and navigation links.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, single-column vertical flow beneath the persistent top header and full-bleed hero. All casting-related components are stacked in a centered content column with a fixed max-width (approximately 960–1120px) aligned to a 12-column grid, mirroring the mockup’s simple, editorial structure. Global elements (header, hero, sticky action bar) span the full width; functional components (forms, selectors, confirmation) live in the central column for focus and clarity.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, single-column vertical flow beneath the persistent top header and full-bleed hero. All casting-related components are stacked in a centered content column with a fixed max-width (approximately 960–1120px) aligned to a 12-column grid, mirroring the mockup’s simple, editorial structure. Global elements (header, hero, sticky action bar) span the full width; functional components (forms, selectors, confirmation) live in the central column for focus and clarity.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header remains a shallow fixed band at the very top (~10–15% viewport height), followed immediately by a full-width hero band that takes about one third to one half of the first viewport height with the page title and a muted image backdrop. Below the hero, the main form stack occupies the central vertical column, with large vertical gaps (48–64px) between major sections to maintain an airy editorial feel. A sticky action bar sits at the bottom edge of the viewport, about 64–80px tall, reserving space so it doesn’t obscure lower form content.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is intentionally low: each form section is contained in a light, card-like block with 16–24px internal padding on all sides, generous line-height, and clear separation between groups. Navigation and buttons have ample horizontal breathing room (8px vertical, 16–24px horizontal padding), avoiding visual clutter. The confirmation state uses more negative space and larger type to echo the hero’s editorial hierarchy and visually signal completion.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye first moves left-to-right across the fixed header (logo, navigation, language button), then drops down into the full-bleed hero with a centered page title. From there, the eye naturally transitions into the main content column: any status feedback at the very top, then the role selector, followed by the major form sections in sequence. The sticky action bar at the bottom of the viewport anchors the primary action. After submission, the confirmation panel appears prominently near the top of the content column, becoming the new focal point, with its inline navigation links to talent search and messages creating a final horizontal scan before the user exits the flow.

layout.overall_description: The 'Create Casting Call' page sits under the same fixed, shallow header and full-bleed hero as the rest of the site: a narrow top bar with a teal logo on the left and spaced navigation links across to a solid teal language button on the right, followed by a wide hero band featuring a blurred grayscale collage and a centered, two-line editorial page title like 'CREATE CASTING CALL'. The main workflow begins just below this hero within a single, centered content column on a white or very light grey background. At the top of this column, a slim status banner (when present) runs the full width of the column, followed by a role selector block, and then three main form sections (basic details, compensation, visibility) stacked vertically with large, consistent gaps. Each section appears as a clean, white card with 16–24px internal padding, lightly separated from the page background to maintain an airy, magazine-like rhythm. At the bottom of the content stack sits a compact submission controller block, while a full-width sticky action bar hugs the bottom of the viewport, containing the primary teal 'Publish' action and secondary controls. When the casting is successfully submitted, an elegant confirmation panel appears near the top of the content column, using larger typography and increased whitespace to echo the hero’s editorial emphasis. Inside this confirmation state, horizontally separated links invite the user to go directly to talent search or messages, closing the loop from creation to activation within the same visually coherent, spacious layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The initialization loader appears as a semi-transparent white scrim overlaying only the main content column beneath the hero, leaving the fixed header and hero visible in the background. In the center of the viewport, aligned both vertically and horizontally within this column, a minimalist spinner and short loading label are displayed, occupying roughly a small card-sized area (about 25–30% of the column width and 10–15% of the viewport height), with generous empty space around them to preserve the calm, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily fixed overlay anchored to the main content area; it scroll-locks the underlying content while visible and disappears entirely once data is loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The creator role selector is positioned at the top of the main content column, directly below any status banner and just under the lower edge of the hero, with approximately 48px of vertical space separating it from the hero content to create a clear transition. It spans the full width of the central column (about 70–80% of the page width on large screens), centered on the page, and appears as a low-profile card with 16–24px of padding and horizontally arranged role choices. The choices themselves are spaced evenly with generous horizontal gaps, reflecting the navigation spacing from the header but in a more compact, form-ready treatment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical flow of the main column; it scrolls normally with the rest of the form content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The basic details form sits directly below the creator role selector, separated by a vertical gap of roughly 48px to maintain the spacious, editorial rhythm. It occupies the same centered content column, spanning about 8 of 12 grid columns (around 65–70% of the viewport width on desktop) with equal left and right margins for balance. The form is housed within a white card-like block with 24px of internal padding, light shadow or subtle border, and its fields arranged in a simple vertical or two-column grid. Ample vertical spacing (16–24px) between field groups mirrors the airy hero aesthetic and keeps the section feeling calm despite containing the most inputs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked within the main column; it extends vertically as needed but always respects the max-width to avoid edge-to-edge clutter.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The compensation form is placed immediately beneath the basic details form, with a consistent 48–56px vertical gap that makes each section feel like its own editorial block. It matches the width and alignment of the basic details form—centered within the page and spanning roughly 8 of 12 grid columns—with the same white card treatment and 24px padding on all sides. Because this section may contain structured inputs (rates, currencies, options), the internal layout is slightly denser but still follows a clear vertical hierarchy with generous spacing between rows, ensuring it visually reads as the second major chapter in the workflow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical flow; it grows vertically with content while staying within the central column and never overlapping adjacent sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The visibility settings section appears below the compensation form, again separated by about 48px of vertical white space that echoes the section breaks found in editorial layouts. It shares the same centered width and card styling—about 8 of 12 columns, white background, 16–24px internal padding—but is slightly more compact vertically due to fewer fields. Toggle controls and visibility options are arranged in a single column for clarity, with each option row getting enough vertical space (16–20px) so that the section feels light and scannable, reinforcing its role as a clear, discrete decision area near the bottom of the form stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and stacked, aligning with the same vertical grid rhythm; scrolls naturally with other form sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission controller sits at the bottom of the main form stack, directly beneath the visibility settings section with roughly 40–48px of separation, acting as a visual conclusion to the form content just above the sticky action bar. It uses the same centered column width but is visually more minimal: a shallow, full-column row or compact card (about 10–15% of the viewport height) containing final checkboxes (e.g., terms agreements) and any last review notes. Internal spacing remains generous with 16px padding so that, despite being functional, it retains the clean, editorial look and clearly leads the user’s eye down toward the sticky action bar where the actual submission action resides.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, appended to the bottom of the form stack; its position relative to the sticky action bar is fixed so that it is the last piece of scrolled form content before hitting the persistent controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The casting action bar is a full-width horizontal band anchored to the bottom edge of the viewport, visually echoing the fixed header at the top and framing the main content between these two horizontal rails. It is relatively shallow (approximately 64–80px high), with a white background and a subtle top shadow to distinguish it from the page content. Within the bar, content is constrained to the same central max-width as the forms; on the left or center sit secondary actions like 'Save Draft', while on the right the primary teal 'Publish Casting Call' button is given prominent placement and ample padding, mirroring the emphasis of the teal language button in the header. There is a small 16–24px vertical buffer between the bottom of the scrolling form content and the top edge of the action bar so the content does not feel cramped against it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky/fixed to the bottom of the viewport, always visible as the user scrolls through form sections; it does not overlap content but instead the page layout reserves space for it visually.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission feedback appears as a slim, full-width banner at the very top of the main content column, directly below the hero and above the creator role selector, spanning the same central max-width as the forms. It has 16–20px of vertical padding and uses either a light teal-tinted background for success or a soft, muted warning color for errors, with text centered or left-aligned but always with generous horizontal margins to preserve the clean aesthetic. The banner occupies roughly 10–12% of the viewport height at most, and when present, pushes all other content downward while maintaining the large white space between it and the hero so the page doesn’t feel crowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and static within the column flow; it appears and disappears without overlaying content, simply shifting the content stack down or up accordingly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: After a successful submission, the confirmation panel appears prominently near the top of the main content column, starting just below the hero (and any active feedback banner) with generous vertical spacing (48–64px) above and below, so it reads almost like a secondary, in-page hero. The panel is wider and airier than the form sections, spanning approximately 9 of 12 columns (around 75% of the page width) and centered. Inside, a large, serif or bold sans-serif headline celebrates the successful casting creation, with ample top and bottom padding (24–32px) and minimal supporting text, echoing the hero’s big-type, low-density style. The forms may collapse or visually recede below it, but the confirmation panel itself visually dominates this area with its open whitespace and strong typographic presence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column; it appears above or in place of the top-of-stack form sections and scrolls normally, but is designed to be the first major element users see when they return to the page state after submission.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation link to talent search is positioned inside the confirmation panel’s lower area, aligned toward the left side of the panel’s content width. It sits on a horizontal row that forms the panel’s footer, with approximately 24px of top padding separating it from the main confirmation headline and body text. The link is styled as a subtle but clear text link or understated button, with the teal brand color applied to the label and perhaps a simple right-pointing arrow icon, and has comfortable horizontal padding (16–24px) while keeping plenty of negative space around it so it feels like a deliberate, editorial call-to-action rather than a cramped utility link.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the confirmation panel, sharing a horizontal row with the messages navigation link; it scrolls with the panel and retains its position as the first follow-up action on the left.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation link to messages sits on the same horizontal footer row of the confirmation panel as the talent search link but is aligned to the right edge of the panel’s content width, creating a balanced left–right pair. It mirrors the styling of the talent search link—teal text, possibly an accompanying icon, and generous horizontal padding—while maintaining ample whitespace between the two to preserve the calm, editorial feel. This symmetrical placement makes the eye travel horizontally one last time across the page, echoing the header’s layout pattern and giving the user a clear fork between continuing to search talent or managing communication.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the confirmation panel, right-aligned on the same row as the talent search navigation; it scrolls with the panel and remains visually paired with the other follow-up action.

name: Talent Profile Setup
title: Talent Profile Setup
description: Page where new talent users create their profile and set date-based availability so they can appear in search and be booked.

core_features: Load dynamic signup options (talent roles, primary categories, experience levels, languages) using getTalentSignupOptions and bind them to the profile setup form.
Detect whether the current user already has a talent profile using getMyTalentProfile and adjust the page flow (e.g., prefill form or focus on availability).
Allow users to enter core profile information (name, contact email, password, location, primary category, role, experience level, languages, bio) and submit it via registerTalentProfile.
Support a multi-step flow where, after profile registration, the user sets specific date-based availability using an interactive calendar and saves it via setMyTalentAvailability.
Provide a completion step with navigation actions to view the public talent profile (talent_profile.html?id=…) and to open relevant workshops (events.html?event_type=workshop).
user_workflows:
New talent profile creation and availability setup:
1. User navigates to talent_profile_setup.html (e.g., via header link "Join as Talent").
2. On page load, the page calls getTalentSignupOptions to obtain selectable options and getMyTalentProfile to detect if a profile already exists.
3. If no existing profile is found, the page shows Step 1 (Profile Details) with empty fields and dropdowns populated from getTalentSignupOptions.
4. The user fills in required fields (full name, email, password, location, primary category, role, languages) and any optional fields (display name, experience level, bio) and submits the form.
5. The page validates the input and, if valid, calls registerTalentProfile with the entered values.
6. If registerTalentProfile succeeds, the response talent_profile is stored in page state, the URL query parameter step is updated to indicate the availability step (e.g., ?step=availability), and Step 2 (Availability) is shown.
7. The user selects one or more dates on the availability calendar component to mark them as available and clicks a button to save.
8. The page transforms selected dates into the availability_slots parameter and calls setMyTalentAvailability.
9. If setMyTalentAvailability succeeds, the URL query parameter step is updated to indicate completion (e.g., ?step=complete), and the completion panel is displayed, offering navigation to the public profile and workshops.

Returning user who already has a talent profile:
1. User opens talent_profile_setup.html.
