component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan list occupies the dominant space within the pricing section. It sits directly below the control band and the cheapest-plan summary and status indicator, starting approximately 32px beneath them. The plan list spans the full width of the main content column, with its left and right edges aligned precisely with the filters above. On desktop, it uses a three-column grid, with each card occupying roughly one third of the column width minus 24px gutters between cards. Each card is tall and airy, with internal padding of about 24px and generous vertical spacing between rows of cards (24–32px). The first row of cards begins about 24px below the status indicator, preserving the calm, editorial spacing. On tablet, the grid collapses to two columns; on mobile, it becomes a single column with each card stretching to nearly the full width of the content column, maintaining 16–24px vertical spacing between cards. This grid forms a strong visual block that naturally draws the eye downward after scanning the controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static grid within the main content area; it scrolls with the page and alternates visibility with the comparison table based on the view-mode switcher (only one of the two is visible at a time in the main slot).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The basic-plan comparison table shares the same vertical slot as the plan list, appearing in its place when the user switches to comparison mode. It begins at the same vertical position as the top of the plan cards would, directly beneath the summary and status indicator, approximately 32px below them. The table spans the full width of the main content column, aligned with the filters above and the cards in the alternative view. Columns for different plans are distributed evenly across this width, while rows are given generous height (around 48–64px) with ample line spacing to avoid a cramped, spreadsheet feel. There is a clear 24px gap between the table header row and the first data row, echoing the editorial spacing. The entire table block is framed by white space above and below (at least 32px to adjacent sections), allowing the grid of horizontal and vertical lines to feel orderly but not overwhelming. On smaller screens, the table may scroll horizontally within this central column, but it remains visually anchored to the same left and right margins as the rest of the content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that alternates with the plan grid in the main content area; only one of them is visible based on view-mode selection. Scrolls with the content, with optional internal horizontal scrolling on small screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cheapest basic summary appears as a slim, understated card sitting just below the control band and above the main plans area. It is aligned to the left of the content column, with its left edge matching the left edge of the plan cards and filters above. Horizontally, it spans about 40–50 percent of the column width, leaving ample empty space to its right, which reinforces the editorial, asymmetric layout. The card has subtle elevation or a light border, internal padding of around 16–24px, and generous breathing room above and below: roughly 24px above (from the controls) and 16–24px below (before the status indicator and plan list or table). This makes it feel like a highlighted note or editor’s pick rather than a heavy callout. On smaller viewports, the card expands to nearly full width while retaining at least 16px of horizontal padding from the container edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static summary panel anchored above the main plans area; scrolls normally with the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plans fetch status indicator is a small, lightweight element placed directly below the cheapest basic summary and just above the main plan area. It aligns with the left edge of the content column, sitting about 8–12px under the summary card, and occupies only as much width as its text or spinner requires (typically less than one third of the column). When active, it may show a subtle spinner and short status text in a muted gray tone, ensuring it is visible but not visually dominant. There is a 16–24px vertical gap between this indicator and the top of the plan grid or comparison table beneath it, so that the main content does not crowd it. When no status needs to be shown, this component collapses to zero height, allowing the plan list or table to sit closer to the summary without breaking the overall spacing rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline status element in the flow above the main plans area; appears and disappears without affecting the alignment of surrounding components, and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout session handler primarily operates behind the scenes, but when a checkout is initiated, it manifests as a centered overlay that temporarily becomes the primary focal point. This overlay sits above all other content, covering the viewport with a soft, semi-transparent dark layer while presenting a clean, white dialog in the center. The dialog occupies roughly 50–60 percent of the viewport width on desktop and about 60–70 percent of the height, floating with generous margins from all sides. Internally, it uses the same spacing system (24–32px padding) and typography hierarchy as the rest of the site, with teal accents on the confirm or continue button. The background pricing content remains visible but dimmed, reinforcing the sense that the user is still within the same editorial world while focusing their attention on completing the transaction.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as a modal overlay fixed to the center of the viewport, above all other components, with a full-screen dimmed backdrop; does not scroll with the underlying page while active, but may scroll internally if content exceeds the dialog height.

name: Subscription Checkout
title: Subscription Checkout
description: Multi-step checkout page to confirm the selected subscription plan on a monthly billing frequency, collect billing details, and show a review summary before submitting the subscription.

core_features: Display the current SubscriptionCheckoutSession for the provided checkoutId, including the associated SubscriptionPlan and the enforced monthly billing frequency.
Show a non-editable plan confirmation area so the user can verify the selected SubscriptionPlan and that billing is set to monthly.
Provide a billing and account details form bound to SubscriptionCheckoutSession fields, allowing the user to enter or update company name, contact email, and billing address information.
Validate billing inputs (at minimum contact_email, plus any additional fields required by business rules) before allowing the user to proceed.
Use updateSubscriptionCheckoutBilling to persist billing details for the current checkoutId when the user submits the billing form.
Use proceedSubscriptionCheckoutToReview to move the checkout session into the review step and retrieve a summary containing plan, billing_frequency, price_per_interval, and currency.

Display a review summary step showing the selected SubscriptionPlan, enforced monthly billing_frequency, and the calculated price_per_interval for that monthly interval, so the user can verify all details.
Allow the user to navigate back to the billing details step from the review step without losing the persisted billing data in the SubscriptionCheckoutSession.
Provide a Change plan action from the review step that navigates back to plans.html with the required plan_type URL parameter.
user_workflows:
1) Landing on Subscription Checkout with a checkoutId:
   - User arrives at subscription_checkout.html with a checkoutId URL parameter.
   - The page reads checkoutId from the URL and binds it to the current SubscriptionCheckoutSession.
   - Based on the session status (e.g., in_progress or review, as supplied by the server), the page sets the current step: either Billing Details or Review.
   - The plan confirmation area displays key fields from the associated SubscriptionPlan (via plan_id) and indicates that billing is monthly.
   - If checkoutId is missing or the session cannot be loaded, the page enters an error state and can provide navigation back to the plans page.

2) Filling in billing details and proceeding to review:
   - The user completes or edits company_name, contact_email, and billing address fields in the billing form.
   - The user clicks a primary action such as “Review subscription”.
   - Client-side validation checks that required fields, including at least contact_email, are present and correctly formatted. Additional billing fields may be treated as required according to business rules.
   - If validation fails, errors are shown inline and the interface does not call any API.
   - If validation succeeds, the page calls updateSubscriptionCheckoutBilling with checkoutId and the current form values.
   - If updateSubscriptionCheckoutBilling returns success = true, the page immediately calls proceedSubscriptionCheckoutToReview with the same checkoutId.
   - When proceedSubscriptionCheckoutToReview returns, the page updates local state with the returned status and summary (plan, billing_frequency, price_per_interval, currency), and moves the user to the Review step.
   - If either interface returns an error or a non-success status, the page shows an error message and remains on the billing step.

3) Reviewing the subscription summary:
   - On entering the Review step, the page uses the summary from proceedSubscriptionCheckoutToReview to render the chosen plan details, billing_frequency, and price_per_interval.
   - The page checks that summary.billing_frequency is monthly; if not, it displays an error message and may disable confirmation actions until the issue is resolved or the user changes the plan.
   - The review step also displays the billing details stored in SubscriptionCheckoutSession (company_name, contact_email, billing address) for user verification.
   - The user can click an Edit billing details action to return to the billing step. The billing form is re-populated from the existing SubscriptionCheckoutSession values.
   - When satisfied, the user clicks a confirmation action (e.g., “Confirm subscription”), which submits the checkout for final processing to a server endpoint outside the scope of the provided interfaces.

4) Changing the selected plan from review:
   - While on the Review step, the user can click a Change plan link or button.
   - The page navigates to plans.html, appending the required URL parameter plan_type=talent_scouting, so the user can select a different subscription plan.
   - After the user selects a new plan on plans.html, a new SubscriptionCheckoutSession can be created and the user is redirected back to subscription_checkout.html with a new checkoutId URL parameter.

5) Refreshing or revisiting the page with an existing session:
   - If the user refreshes the page or returns later to subscription_checkout.html?checkoutId=..., the checkoutId is again parsed from the URL.
   - The server may provide the current SubscriptionCheckoutSession and status. If status is review, the page opens directly to the Review step and can optionally re-call proceedSubscriptionCheckoutToReview if a fresh summary is needed.
   - If status is in_progress, the page opens on the billing details step, pre-populating the form with any saved values.
   - If status is completed or cancelled, the page enters a read-only or error state and may display a message that this checkout session is no longer active.
interactions: Clicking between steps using navigation controls (e.g., Back to billing from the review step) updates the local step state but keeps the same checkoutId in the URL.

Typing into billing fields (company_name, contact_email, billing address components) updates local form state for SubscriptionCheckoutSession and is not persisted until updateSubscriptionCheckoutBilling is called.
Submitting the billing form triggers validation and, upon success, an updateSubscriptionCheckoutBilling call followed by proceedSubscriptionCheckoutToReview.
Errors returned from updateSubscriptionCheckoutBilling or proceedSubscriptionCheckoutToReview are surfaced to the user via an error message component bound to the current step.
Clicking Change plan from the review step triggers navigation to plans.html with the query parameter plan_type=talent_scouting.
Clicking Edit billing details on the review step sets the current step back to Billing Details, reusing the same checkoutId and existing SubscriptionCheckoutSession data.
Clicking the final Confirm subscription button on the review step submits the current checkoutId and any necessary data to a server endpoint (outside these interfaces) to finalize the subscription.
If the page detects that checkoutId is missing from the URL, an interaction is shown to redirect the user back to the plans page so they can start a new checkout.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state driver for this page is the checkoutId URL parameter. On initialization, the page reads checkoutId from the query string (e.g., subscription_checkout.html?checkoutId=...). If checkoutId is not present, the page cannot bind a SubscriptionCheckoutSession and must display an error or redirect the user back to plans.html.

Once checkoutId is available, the page treats it as the identifier for all subsequent calls to updateSubscriptionCheckoutBilling and proceedSubscriptionCheckoutToReview. The current step (Billing Details or Review) is derived from the SubscriptionCheckoutSession.status value as supplied by the server or by the last interface response. A status of in_progress maps to the Billing Details step; a status of review maps to the Review step. If the status is completed or cancelled, the page switches to a terminal state (e.g., read-only or error), where action buttons are disabled.

The plan details shown in the plan summary components are linked through SubscriptionCheckoutSession.plan_id to a SubscriptionPlan. The billing_frequency is enforced as monthly at the session level (e.g., configured when the session was created). When the page receives the summary object from proceedSubscriptionCheckoutToReview, it verifies that summary.billing_frequency equals the expected monthly value. If not, the UI indicates a configuration error and prevents final confirmation until the issue is corrected (for example, by changing the plan or re-creating the checkout session from the plans page).

The page does not use localStorage or persistent client-side storage. All durable information is tied to the server-side SubscriptionCheckoutSession referenced by checkoutId, plus transient in-memory UI state (e.g., the active step and local form input buffer). Reloading the page with the same checkoutId re-derives the view solely from server-provided session data and, if invoked, from fresh responses to the assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: Controls which step of the subscription checkout is active (Billing Details vs Review) based on checkoutId from the URL and SubscriptionCheckoutSession.status. Handles invalid or terminal session states and coordinates transitions after interface calls.
Displays a concise summary of the selected SubscriptionPlan for the current checkoutId and confirms that billing is set to monthly. Used on both the Billing Details and Review steps.

Collects and validates billing and account information (company_name, contact_email, billing address fields) for the current SubscriptionCheckoutSession and persists it via updateSubscriptionCheckoutBilling before moving to the Review step.

Primary action on the billing step that validates the billing form, calls updateSubscriptionCheckoutBilling, and then calls proceedSubscriptionCheckoutToReview to obtain the summary and transition the checkout into the Review step.

Displays the final review summary for the subscription, including plan details, billing_frequency, and price_per_interval returned by proceedSubscriptionCheckoutToReview, along with the billing details from SubscriptionCheckoutSession so the user can confirm all information.
Shows global error or status messages during the checkout flow, such as missing checkoutId, interface errors, or incompatible billing_frequency state.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a full-width container for the hero and a fixed max-width inner container for header content and navigation links.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow anchored in a single central content column. Directly under the global header, a full-width but shallow checkout step controller creates a horizontal band, echoing the mockup’s narrow header. Below that, the core checkout content sits in a centered max-width container divided into two columns on desktop: a dominant left column for the billing details form and a narrower right column for the plan confirmation summary. The review summary occupies a full-width block beneath these columns. The error notification sits as a full-width bar at the very top of the checkout content, pushing everything down when visible. On mobile, all elements collapse into a single vertical stack while preserving generous whitespace.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow anchored in a single central content column. Directly under the global header, a full-width but shallow checkout step controller creates a horizontal band, echoing the mockup’s narrow header. Below that, the core checkout content sits in a centered max-width container divided into two columns on desktop: a dominant left column for the billing details form and a narrower right column for the plan confirmation summary. The review summary occupies a full-width block beneath these columns. The error notification sits as a full-width bar at the very top of the checkout content, pushing everything down when visible. On mobile, all elements collapse into a single vertical stack while preserving generous whitespace.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The step controller occupies a shallow horizontal band (roughly 10–15% of the initial viewport height including its top and bottom padding). The main checkout container spans approximately 80–90% of the viewport width up to a fixed max-width (around 1040–1120px), centered. Within this, the billing form takes about 60–65% of the width on the left, while the plan summary and, when needed, a compact version of the review summary sit in the remaining 35–40% on the right. Vertical spacing between major sections (step controller, form block, review summary block) uses the larger spacing scale (48–64px), while internal spacing within cards and forms uses 16–24px. The error notification bar spans the full browser width but is relatively shallow (48–56px tall) to avoid feeling heavy.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is deliberately low and calm, mirroring the editorial aesthetic. Each module (step controller, plan summary, form, review summary, notification) reads as a distinct block with clear breathing room around it. Form fields are arranged predominantly in a single column, with only occasional two-up rows for smaller fields, and each field stack has comfortable vertical spacing (16–24px). Typography for headings and step labels uses larger sizes and generous line-height, and there are no dense tables or overly compact elements. Teal accents are used sparingly for key actions (review button) and status highlights (current step, active error bar), while the rest remains grayscale and white.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the top-left with the global logo (outside this spec), then immediately enters the checkout area via the horizontally oriented step controller. From left to right, the user reads their current position in the flow, then naturally drops down to the dominant left-hand billing form column. Peripheral vision keeps the right-hand plan summary in view, reinforcing what they are buying. At the bottom of the form, the strong teal review trigger button pulls attention next. Once activated, the flow continues straight down to the full-width review summary, where the user scans from top to bottom before final confirmation. If errors occur, the high-contrast notification bar briefly becomes the first focal point at the top, then the eye is guided back down to the affected fields.

layout.overall_description: The subscription checkout page sits beneath the site’s fixed, minimal header and inherits its clean, editorial character. The main checkout experience is framed within a soft, light backdrop—either a very subtle grayscale texture or a solid off-white—so that a centered, max-width content column reads like a refined magazine spread. At the very top of this column, a slim, full-width strip shows the checkout steps in large, clear typography, with the current step highlighted in teal, mirroring the language switch button from the mockup. Below this, the layout opens into a spacious two-column composition: on the left, a tall, airy billing form with large labels and plenty of breathing room; on the right, a compact plan summary card with concise, well-spaced lines of information about the selected subscription. Both columns align vertically at the top and sit inside the same horizontal band, separated by generous gutter space. At the bottom-left of the form, a bold teal button—styled similarly to the mockup’s FRANÇAIS button—acts as the primary call to action to continue to review. When the user advances, a full-width review summary section appears beneath the columns, returning to a single-column editorial rhythm with grouped information blocks and generous vertical spacing. If something goes wrong, a full-width notification bar appears just under the global header and above the step controller, drawing attention without disrupting the overall calm, high-end feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout step controller is positioned at the very top of the checkout content area, directly below the global site header and any visible error notification bar. It spans the full width of the central max-width container (approximately 80–90% of the viewport width, centered) and sits within a shallow horizontal band about 72–96px tall including padding. The controller presents the 2–3 steps (e.g., Plan, Billing Details, Review) in a single horizontal row aligned from left to right, with each step label set in larger, serif or semi-serif type to echo the editorial hero headline from the mockup. Steps are evenly spaced across the width, with the current step highlighted using the teal accent color and possibly a subtle underline or dot, while remaining steps are rendered in a muted gray. There is generous white space above and below the labels (16–24px padding), ensuring the band feels light rather than dense. On mobile, the controller remains at the top, but steps may wrap into two lines or condense into a numbered breadcrumb while maintaining comfortable spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The step controller is positioned statically within the document flow by default but can become sticky: as the user scrolls down the long billing form, it pins to the top of the viewport just beneath the global header (and any notification bar), maintaining a shallow fixed band that constantly reminds the user of their progress.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan confirmation summary sits within the main checkout content container, aligned to the right side to form the secondary column. On desktop and larger tablets, it occupies roughly 35–40% of the container width and aligns vertically with the top of the billing details form to its left. The card’s content is arranged in a vertically stacked layout: plan name at the top in a slightly larger, elegant serif style; price and billing frequency beneath in bold but compact type; followed by a short list of key inclusions or notes in lighter-weight text. The card has internal padding of 24px on all sides and sits on a clean white or very light surface with either a subtle 1px gray border or soft shadow, allowing it to feel like a refined “sidebar article” without clutter. There is ample space (32–40px) between this card and the left-hand form column, forming a generous gutter. On mobile, this summary moves above the billing details form, spanning 100% of the container width, with extra vertical margin (24–32px) beneath it to clearly separate it from the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On taller desktop viewports, the plan summary panel can adopt a sticky behavior within its column: as the user scrolls through the longer billing form on the left, the summary remains pinned within the top portion of the right column until its bottom edge reaches the bottom of the form section. This maintains constant visibility of the plan without overlapping or leaving its column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The billing details form is the primary vertical block in the main content area, positioned directly below the checkout step controller with a generous top margin of approximately 48px. It forms the left column of the two-column layout and occupies around 60–65% of the max-width container on desktop. The form uses a predominantly single-column field layout: each label and input pair is stacked vertically, with 16–24px of vertical spacing between field groups to maintain the calm, editorial rhythm. For shorter, related fields (e.g., city and postal code), two fields may be placed side by side within the same row, each taking about 48% of the form width with a small gutter, while still preserving adequate breathing room. Section headings within the form (like “Billing Address”, “Payment Details”) are set in slightly larger, serif type with generous top margins (32–40px) to act as visual anchors. The form content itself sits on a clean, minimally styled background—either white or the same base as the page—with ample internal padding at the top and bottom (24–32px) to keep fields from touching neighboring components. On mobile, the form expands to 100% of the container width as the only major block beneath the plan summary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The billing details form scrolls naturally within the page, retaining its position as the main vertical content driver. It does not fix or float, ensuring a predictable, document-like reading experience, while the sticky step controller and optional sticky plan summary remain visible around it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The review trigger action button is anchored at the bottom of the billing details form, acting as the culmination of the left column’s content. It is left-aligned with the form fields, sitting approximately 32px below the last input or checkbox group, and has a generous amount of whitespace beneath it (at least 48px) before any subsequent content, reinforcing its importance. The button itself is rendered as a solid teal rectangle with rounded but not overly pill-like corners, echoing the bold teal language switch button from the mockup. It uses prominent, uppercase or small-caps text (e.g., “REVIEW & CONFIRM”) in a clean sans-serif or understated serif, with approximately 8px vertical and 24px horizontal padding, making it visually substantial without feeling bulky. On wider screens, the button’s width is intrinsic to its label (not full-width), but never appears cramped; on mobile, it can expand to 100% width of the content column while maintaining tall vertical padding and adequate margin from screen edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The review trigger remains in the normal document flow at the end of the billing form. When clicked, it transitions the interface state managed by the step controller: either scrolling smoothly to reveal the review summary panel below or switching to a distinct review step where the form becomes read-only or collapses while the review summary panel becomes prominent.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The review summary panel appears as a distinct section beneath the main form and plan summary columns, returning the layout to a single-column, editorial-style band. On desktop, it spans the full width of the central container (aligned with both the form and the plan summary above) and is separated from them by generous vertical spacing of about 48–64px, ensuring it reads as a new chapter in the flow. The panel’s content is organized into clearly labeled blocks: one for plan and pricing, one for billing and payment details, and one for legal/terms. Each block’s heading is set in large, elegant type, with 24–32px separation between blocks. Key values (total price, renewal date) can be right-aligned within their rows to subtly echo the horizontal navigation feel from the mockup while preserving an overall vertical reading direction. The panel rests on a white background with 24–32px padding all around and may use faint dividers (1px gray rules) between sections to maintain clarity without clutter. On mobile, it stacks seamlessly below the form (or replaces it in a dedicated review step), still occupying the full width with the same airy spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The review summary panel is revealed conditionally, controlled by the checkout step state. In a multi-step flow, it may replace the form in the viewport (with the step controller indicating the current 'Review' step), while in a single-page progressive disclosure approach it appears below the form and may be scrolled into view automatically after the review trigger is pressed. It remains static within the page once visible, encouraging users to scroll and scan top-to-bottom before final submission.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout error notification manifests as a full-width horizontal bar at the very top of the checkout area, immediately below the global site header and above the checkout step controller. It spans the entire browser width but centers its message within the same max-width content container used for the rest of the page, creating a consistent alignment. The bar is relatively shallow (approximately 48–56px in height) with 12–16px vertical padding, and uses a high-contrast yet restrained style: a white or very light background with a prominent teal left border or top border, plus concise error text in dark gray or near-black, possibly accompanied by a small icon on the left. The text is left-aligned within the container but given generous horizontal padding (16–24px) so it never feels cramped. There is a clear visual separation (8–16px margin) between this bar and the step controller below, maintaining the layered banding effect seen in the original hero-header relationship.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: When present, the notification bar pushes the rest of the content downward instead of overlaying it, preserving the calm, editorial structure of the page. It remains fixed in place relative to the document flow (not sticky), appearing and disappearing based on error state. In moments of error, it temporarily becomes the first focal band underneath the site header before the user’s attention is guided back down to the relevant form fields.

name: Events & Workshops
title: Events & Workshops
description: Browse and filter upcoming events, workshops, and training sessions, with search, date and location filters, sorting, and navigation to detailed event pages or local talent search.

core_features: Load and display a paginated list of upcoming events using the getEvents interface, showing key details from each Event record.
Filter events by location, event type, and date range using URL parameters mapped to getEvents parameters.
Search events by title keywords and keep the search term in sync with the URL and getEvents.title_query parameter.
Sort event results (e.g., by date) using the sort_by URL parameter and pass it to getEvents.
Paginate through event results using page and page_size URL parameters, passed through to getEvents.
Navigate to an individual event detail page when an event result is selected, sending the Event.id as the id URL parameter.
Navigate to the talent search page for the location of a selected event, passing the Event.location_display as the location URL parameter.
Handle empty or error states for event search (no results or interface errors) without relying on static fallback data.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Arriving with pre-set filters from another page
1. User clicks an 'Events' link elsewhere (e.g., index.html) that includes URL parameters such as location, event_type, title_query, start_date, or end_date.
2. Events & Workshops page reads current URL parameters on load.
3. Page constructs a getEvents request using the URL parameters (location, event_type, title_query, start_date, end_date, sort_by, page, page_size).
4. Page calls getEvents and receives a list of Event results along with total, page, and page_size.
5. The filter/search form components initialize their visible values from the URL parameters so the user sees which filters are active.
6. The results list component renders event cards based on the Event objects returned.
7. User can refine filters or search further as needed.

Workflow 2: Filtering and searching within the page
1. User adjusts filter controls (e.g., changes location, selects an event type, sets a date range, enters a title keyword).
2. When the user submits the filter/search form, the page constructs an updated query string reflecting all filter values (location, event_type, title_query, start_date, end_date) and resets page to the first page.
3. The page updates the URL parameters to match the new filter state.
4. The page triggers a new getEvents call with the updated parameters.
5. On response, the results list updates to show the filtered events; pagination controls reflect page and total from the response.

Workflow 3: Sorting results
1. User changes the sort option in the sort control (e.g., selects a different sort order for dates).
2. The sort control updates the sort_by URL parameter, resets page to the first page, and leaves other filter parameters unchanged.
3. The page calls getEvents with the updated sort_by and current filter parameters.
4. The results list re-renders in the new sort order, and pagination is updated according to the new results.

Workflow 4: Pagination
1. User clicks a pagination control (next page, previous page, or direct page number).
2. The pagination component updates the page URL parameter (and optionally page_size if changed) while preserving all other filter and sort parameters.
3. The page calls getEvents with the new page (and page_size) values.
4. The results list updates to show the events corresponding to the requested page.

Workflow 5: Viewing event details
1. User clicks anywhere on an event card designated as a link.
2. The click handler reads the Event.id from the selected result item.
3. The browser navigates to event_detail.html with id set to the clicked Event.id in the query string.
4. The event_detail.html page can optionally pass back a location filter or other parameters if the user navigates back to events.html later.

Workflow 6: Finding local talent from an event
1. User clicks the 'Find local talent' action on an event card.
2. The click handler reads the Event.location_display (or a derived city/state combination) for that event.
3. The browser navigates to talent_search.html with location set to the selected event’s location in the query string.
4. The talent_search.html page initializes its filters using this location parameter.

Workflow 7: Handling no results
1. User applies filters that produce zero results.
2. getEvents returns an empty results array with total value indicating zero.
3. The results list component shows a functional empty-state message that uses only the current filter parameters (no hardcoded event examples) and offers a control to clear or adjust filters.
4. If the user chooses to clear filters, the form resets filter fields to a neutral state, updates the URL parameters accordingly (removing or clearing filter-related params), and triggers a new getEvents call.
interactions:
Filter/search form interactions:
- User types into a title search input; the component stores the current value locally until the user submits the form.
- User selects or edits the location filter; the component updates its internal form state.
- User chooses an event type from a selection control; the component updates its internal form state.
- User selects a start_date and optionally end_date via date-picker or text fields; the component updates its internal form state.
- User submits the form; the submit handler constructs a URL query object including all filters and sets page to the first page before updating the URL and triggering getEvents.

Sort control interactions:
- User selects a sort option from a sort control.
- The sort control fires an onChange event, updating the sort_by URL parameter, resetting page to the first page, and leaving other parameters unchanged, then triggers getEvents.

Pagination interactions:
- User clicks a next/previous page button or a specific page number.
- The pagination component fires an onPageChange event that updates the page URL parameter (and page_size if changed) and triggers getEvents.
- If the user changes page_size (if such control exists), the page_size URL parameter is updated, page is reset to the first page, and getEvents is called.

Event card interactions:
- User clicks the main area of an event card (e.g., title or explicit 'View details' control).
- The card’s onClick handler navigates to event_detail.html with id set to the Event.id.
- User clicks the 'Find local talent' action on the event card.
- The onClick handler reads Event.location_display (or equivalent) and navigates to talent_search.html with location set to that value.

Initial load and URL change interactions:
- On initial page load or when the URL changes, a controller component reads all supported URL parameters (location, event_type, title_query, start_date, end_date, sort_by, page, page_size).
- The controller normalizes/validates parameter values (e.g., ensuring page is a positive number, dates are valid; falling back to defaults if not) without introducing static event data.
- The controller calls getEvents with the normalized parameters and passes the results to the results list and pagination components.

Error/empty result interactions:
- If getEvents returns an error or cannot be reached, the page shows an error state that allows the user to retry the request without using static placeholder event data.
- If getEvents returns zero results, an empty-state component appears and offers actions like adjusting filters or clearing all filters; these actions modify the URL parameters and re-trigger getEvents.
page_functionality.state_logic: The URL query string is the single source of truth for the Events & Workshops page state. On page load, the controller reads location, event_type, title_query, start_date, end_date, sort_by, page, and page_size from the URL. Missing or invalid values are normalized to implementation-defined defaults (e.g., first page, a default sort order) but no event contents are hardcoded. After normalization, the controller calls getEvents with these parameters. All interactive components (filters, search, sorting, pagination) update the URL parameters on user interaction before triggering new data loads via getEvents. For example, submitting the filter form updates location, event_type, title_query, start_date, end_date, resets page to the first page, leaves page_size and sort_by unchanged (unless explicitly changed), and then calls getEvents. Changing sort_by updates only the sort_by and page parameters and re-calls getEvents. Pagination updates page (and page_size if applicable) while preserving filter and sort parameters. Navigations to event_detail.html and talent_search.html use values from the current Event results to set outgoing URL parameters (id and location, respectively) without persisting any state outside the URL.

components.functionality: Reads and normalizes URL parameters on load and on URL changes, constructs the parameter object for getEvents, triggers data loading, and distributes the results and current state to child components.

Provides controls for users to specify location, event_type, title_query, start_date, and end_date filters, initializes its values from current URL parameters, and updates the URL state and triggers getEvents when submitted or cleared.
Allows users to choose a sort order for events (e.g., by date or other supported criteria), reflects the current sort_by URL parameter, and updates sort_by and page when changed.
Displays the list of Event results returned by getEvents using one event card per Event object, and routes click interactions to navigation handlers or other components.
Represents a single Event item, showing essential details from the Event model, and provides actions to view event details or find local talent for the event location.

Displays pagination controls based on total, page, and page_size from getEvents, allows navigating between pages, and updates the URL parameters and triggers getEvents when the user changes pages or page size.
Displays when getEvents returns zero results, using only the current filters (from URL parameters) to inform the user and providing actions to clear or modify filters.
Displays when getEvents fails or returns an error, and offers a retry option that reuses the current URL parameters to call getEvents again.
Shows a loading state while getEvents is in progress and hides itself when results or an error are available.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a full-width container for the hero and a fixed max-width inner container for header content and navigation links.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: fixed global header + full-bleed hero at the very top, then a single main content column for Events & Workshops. Inside this column, place a compact filter/search band at the top (search on the left, sort on the right), followed by a vertically stacked list of event cards, and pagination at the bottom. Empty, error, and loading states occupy the same central list area, replacing the cards when active rather than adding extra layout complexity.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: fixed global header + full-bleed hero at the very top, then a single main content column for Events & Workshops. Inside this column, place a compact filter/search band at the top (search on the left, sort on the right), followed by a vertically stacked list of event cards, and pagination at the bottom. Empty, error, and loading states occupy the same central list area, replacing the cards when active rather than adding extra layout complexity.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Keep the header relatively shallow and the hero tall, taking most of the first viewport as in the mockup. Below the hero, use a centered max-width container (~1040–1200px) for all Events components. Allocate a low but visually clear band for the filters (about 15–20% of the content area above the fold), and reserve the bulk of vertical space for the event results list. Maintain generous vertical spacing: 48–64px from hero to filter band, 24–32px between filter band and list, and 32–48px between list and pagination, reflecting the airy, editorial feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep density low and calm. Use a single-column layout for events on desktop with full-width cards inside the content container, generous internal padding (16–24px) per card, and 24–32px gaps between them. The filter bar elements are generously spaced with breathing room to avoid a utilitarian, cramped look. Empty and error states should be visually simple, text-forward, and surrounded by white space, echoing the hero’s simplicity and focus on typography.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Reinforce the mockup’s flow: the eye first travels horizontally across the fixed header (logo to navigation to language button), then drops vertically to the full-bleed hero with its large centered title (e.g., ‘Events & Workshops’). From the hero, the eye moves straight down into the centered filter/search band, first encountering the left-aligned search field and then the right-aligned sort control. From there, attention flows down through the vertical column of event cards. Teal accents (e.g., active filters, key dates, links) punctuate the grayscale/white layout, guiding the eye down the list and finally to the centered pagination at the bottom.

layout.overall_description: Below the fixed global header and a full-bleed hero with a soft, grayscale background image and a bold, centered ‘Events & Workshops’ title, the page transitions into a clean white content area framed by generous margins. A single, centered content column (max-width around 1100px) anchors all Events-related components. At the top of this column, a horizontal filter band spans most of the width: a large, left-aligned search field dominates two-thirds of the row, while a compact sort control aligns to the right, both breathing in ample white space above and below. Directly beneath, a vertical stack of minimal, editorial event cards runs down the page, each card full-width within the column and separated by comfortable gaps, creating a rhythm that’s easy to scan. Teal accents highlight key interactive elements and important metadata, standing out against the predominantly grayscale imagery and white background. When there are no results, or when errors or loading states occur, the central list area is taken over by a single, calm message or indicator centered within the same column, preserving the strong vertical axis. At the bottom, a centered pagination control closes the composition, with a generous margin above the footer, maintaining the overall quiet, high-end magazine aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This state-controller is non-visual and does not render any interface elements. Conceptually, it sits at the top of the Events & Workshops content hierarchy, logically just inside the main content section beneath the hero. It does not occupy any width or height in the layout, nor does it affect spacing directly; instead, it governs the URL and synchronizes filter, sort, and pagination states for the visible components that occupy the central content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered, purely functional; no spatial footprint, does not scroll or position itself in the visual layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form is the first visible functional element after the hero. It is positioned at the top of the main white content area, centered horizontally within a max-width container that spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport on desktop (capped at about 1100px). Vertically, it sits with a generous margin of approximately 48–64px below the bottom of the hero, creating a clear visual break. The search input itself forms the left section of a horizontal filter bar, occupying about 60–65% of the container’s width. It is left-aligned, with a tall input field and ample internal padding, expressing the editorial style (large, clear typography, lots of breathing room). There is 16–24px of padding above and below within this band, ensuring the search area feels like a distinct yet understated section. On smaller viewports, the search form shifts to span nearly 100% of the container width and sits on its own row above the sort control, preserving clarity and touch-friendly spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows with the page scroll as a standard block element. It is not sticky; it scrolls up and out of view along with the content to preserve the calm, uninterrupted vertical reading experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control shares the same horizontal band as the search form on desktop. Within the same centered content container, it is positioned to the right of the search field, aligned along the same baseline. It occupies the remaining 35–40% of the row, right-aligned so that its label and dropdown (or segmented control) nestle against the container’s right edge while still leaving comfortable left/right padding (16–24px) inside the container. The visual density is low: a single, clearly labeled control with ample whitespace around it, matching the simplicity of the navigation in the header. On tablet and mobile, the sort control drops below the search form, spanning close to the full container width but remaining slightly narrower than the container to keep side margins intact. A vertical gap of about 16px separates it from the search form when stacked, maintaining the sense of a unified filter band rather than two isolated components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as part of the filter band, scrolling normally with the page. It does not stick to the top, allowing the hero and header to remain the primary fixed reference points.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results list is positioned directly beneath the filter/search band, still within the same centered content container. There is a vertical gap of roughly 24–32px between the bottom edge of the filter band and the top of the first event card, reinforcing separation while keeping the relationship clear. The list stretches to the full width of the container (about 70–80% of viewport width on desktop), but each card inside is framed with interior padding and generous vertical spacing to maintain the clean, editorial aesthetic. Events are presented in a single column: cards are stacked one after another, with 24–32px of vertical space between each. This single-column approach creates a strong vertical reading path, mirroring the shift from hero to headline in the mockup. The list area extends down the page, consuming the majority of the content height; on long pages, it becomes the primary scrolling area, framed by consistent left and right margins. On smaller screens, the list simply narrows with the container, always remaining centered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block that grows vertically with the number of events. It forms the main scrollable content region but does not introduce independent scrolling; it scrolls with the page as a whole.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each event-result-card sits inside the results list as a full-width card within the centered content column. Horizontally, the card stretches almost the entire width of the container, with consistent 16–24px internal padding on all sides, giving the event details ample breathing room. Cards are separated vertically by 24–32px of whitespace, so each reads as a distinct editorial block, similar to an article excerpt in a magazine layout. The background is white with a subtle shadow or very light border to gently lift the card from the page without feeling heavy. Key text elements—such as the event title and date—use larger serif or clean sans-serif type with generous line spacing, echoing the hero’s typography hierarchy, while teal accents highlight interactive elements like ‘View Details’ links or key metadata. Imagery, if present, is kept in grayscale or low-saturation to match the overall aesthetic, typically aligned to the left or top of the card, with text flowing to the right or beneath. The card height is flexible, expanding to fit content but generally occupying about 15–25% of the viewport height on desktop, depending on content length.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows within the results list with no special behavior. Cards maintain consistent margins and padding across breakpoints, with their width simply adjusting to the container size on smaller screens while staying centered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination control is placed directly beneath the last event card in the results list, still inside the centered content container. A vertical gap of about 32–48px separates it from the final card to clearly signal a transition from content to navigation. The control itself is horizontally centered and relatively compact, occupying roughly 40–60% of the container width at most, so it feels like a focused navigation element rather than a full-width bar. Individual page numbers, next/previous arrows, or a ‘Load more’ button are spaced using the same 8–16px increments to maintain the airy rhythm. Teal accents highlight the current page or primary pagination action. Below the pagination, there is a generous margin of about 64px before the start of the footer or next major site section, preserving the sense of a deliberate ending to the Events list area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the bottom of the results; it scrolls into view as the user reaches the end of the list and does not remain sticky. Its position is always tied directly to the content height of the results list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state occupies the same spatial footprint as the results list when there are no matching events. It appears in the exact location where the first event card would normally begin—directly beneath the filter/search band, with the same 24–32px vertical gap. Within the centered content container, the empty-state content (icon/illustration and text) is horizontally centered and constrained to a narrower column (around 50–60% of the container width) to maintain focus and readability. A prominent headline (e.g., ‘No events found’) is set in large, calm typography similar to the hero title style but scaled down, with a short supporting sentence below. There is ample whitespace above and below the message—enough that the empty state feels intentional and calm rather than abrupt. On taller viewports, the empty state may appear to float in the upper-middle of the visible area as the user scrolls, but it always remains anchored to the content flow beneath the filters.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displayed conditionally in place of the results list; when active, it replaces the list content in the layout. It scrolls normally with the page and does not overlay other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state also appears in the central content column directly beneath the filter/search band, occupying the same region the results list would use. It is aligned to the same left and right edges of the content container, but the message block itself can be slightly left-aligned to feel more like an urgent notice within the editorial content. A concise error headline (e.g., ‘Something went wrong’) appears in bold, legible type, with a short explanation and a teal-accented retry action or link underneath. Vertical spacing around this block mirrors the empty state: 24–32px above from the filter band and at least 48px below before any subsequent content or footer, ensuring the error reads as a single, clear interruption rather than clutter. The visual treatment is minimal—no loud colors beyond subtle teal accents—maintaining the sophisticated, calm aesthetic even when problems occur.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the results list region when an error occurs. It is not an overlay; it pushes down subsequent content as needed and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading indicator is positioned within the bounds of the results list area, centered both horizontally and, when feasible, vertically within the visible portion of the content column beneath the filters. On initial load, it appears in the space where the first event card would be, approximately 24–32px below the filter/search band. The indicator (spinner, subtle animation, or text like ‘Loading events…’) is small and understated, occupying only a small fraction of the container width (around 20–30%), but surrounded by ample whitespace so it remains easy to notice. If loading more events while existing cards are visible, the indicator appears at the bottom of the list, centered, just above the pagination area. The styling stays minimal—thin lines, soft animation, possibly accented with the teal brand color—so it feels in harmony with the refined, editorial visual language.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally within the results list region. It may temporarily replace the list contents on initial load or append to the bottom when loading additional pages. It does not fix itself to the viewport; it scrolls with the content.

name: Event Detail & Registration
title: Event Details & Registration
description: Page to view detailed information for a single event or workshop and register seats with available payment options.

core_features: Load full details for a specific event using getEventDetails with the event ID from the URL.
Display event information including title, description, date and time, location, venue, capacity, pricing, and allowed payment options.
Allow the user to select the number of seats to reserve, constrained by the event’s remaining capacity.
Allow the user to select one of the event’s allowed payment options (e.g., pay_on_site, pay_online, pay_later) as provided by getEventDetails.
Create an event registration using createEventRegistration with the selected seats and payment option.
Show an inline confirmation state with registration details after createEventRegistration succeeds, or display validation/error messages if it fails.
Provide navigation back to all events in the same location and to talent search in related categories, passing location and category via URL parameters.
user_workflows: View event details: (a) User opens event_detail.html with an id URL parameter coming from events.html or index.html. (b) On page load, the page reads id from the URL and calls getEventDetails(eventId=id). (c) When getEventDetails returns, the page shows the event’s title, category, type, description, date/time range, location details, venue (if available), capacity information, pricing per seat and currency (if provided), and the list of allowed payment_options. (d) If the event is not published or cannot be loaded, the page shows an error state instead of the registration form.

Register seats for an event: (a) With event details loaded, the user sees a registration form with a numeric seat selector and a payment option selector populated from the Event.payment_options array. (b) The user selects the desired number of seats; the form validates that the value is at least 1 and does not exceed Event.capacity_remaining when that value is provided. (c) As the user changes the seat count, the form recalculates and displays the total price using Event.price_per_seat and Event.currency when these are available. (d) The user selects a payment option from the allowed list; the form prevents submission if no payment option is selected. (e) The user submits the form, which calls createEventRegistration(eventId=Event.id, seats_reserved=selectedSeats, payment_option=selectedPaymentOption). (f) If the API call succeeds with success=true, the page shows a confirmation state using the returned registration object (e.g., seats_reserved, payment_option, total_price, currency, status). (g) If the API call fails or returns success=false, the page shows the returned message as an error and keeps the form populated so the user can adjust and retry.

Navigate back to events in this city: (a) After viewing the event details, the user can click a text link or button such as 'Back to all events in this city'. (b) The page derives an eventLocation value from the loaded Event (e.g., using Event.location_display or a combination of location_city/location_state/location_country). (c) The page navigates to events.html with a location URL parameter, e.g., events.html?location=eventLocation (no hardcoded location values).

Find talent attending similar workshops: (a) From the event detail view, the user can click a CTA such as 'Find talent attending similar workshops'. (b) The page derives eventLocation as above and a relatedCategory value from the Event data (e.g., Event.category or another appropriate category field). (c) The page navigates to talent_search.html with location and primary_category URL parameters, e.g., talent_search.html?location=eventLocation&primary_category=relatedCategory, without hardcoded values.

Handle error and unavailable states: (a) If the id URL parameter is missing, invalid, or getEventDetails fails, the page shows an error message indicating the event could not be loaded and hides the registration form. (b) If Event.capacity_remaining is zero or the event is otherwise full or not accepting registrations based on business logic derived from Event fields, the registration form is disabled or replaced with a message indicating that registration is closed. (c) If Event.payment_options is empty or missing, the form prevents submission and displays a message explaining that no payment options are currently available.
interactions: Initial page load: Reading the id query parameter from the URL triggers a call to getEventDetails; the result populates all event-related display components and the registration form state.
Seat selector change: When the user changes the number of seats, the page validates the numeric input and updates the displayed total price based on Event.price_per_seat and the selected seat count.
Payment option selection: When the user selects a payment option from those provided by Event.payment_options, the chosen value is stored in form state and used when calling createEventRegistration.

Registration form submission: Submitting the registration form triggers client-side validation (required fields, capacity limits) and then calls createEventRegistration; success or failure responses update the on-page confirmation/error states without navigating away.
Back to all events click: Clicking the 'Back to all events in this city' control constructs a URL to events.html with location set from the loaded Event’s location data and navigates there.

Find similar talent click: Clicking the 'Find talent attending similar workshops' control constructs a URL to talent_search.html with location and primary_category derived from the Event’s location and category, then navigates there.

Retry after error: If createEventRegistration returns an error, the user can adjust seats or payment option and resubmit; the previously entered values remain in the form so the user does not have to re-enter them.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is driven by URL query parameters and API responses. The required id parameter (e.g., event_detail.html?id={Event.id}) determines which Event is loaded. On initial load or when id changes, the page calls getEventDetails(eventId=id) and updates the in-memory view state with the returned Event object. Derived values such as eventLocation and relatedCategory are computed from the loaded Event (for example, using Event.location_display and Event.category) at runtime, not hardcoded, and are only used to construct outgoing navigation URLs like events.html?location={eventLocation} and talent_search.html?location={eventLocation}&primary_category={relatedCategory}. The registration form state (selected seats, selected payment option, submission status, and the resulting EventRegistration object) is held in page state, not persisted via URL or localStorage. However, if needed, the page can safely be reloaded with the same id parameter to re-fetch the same Event details using getEventDetails. No business data such as prices, capacities, locations, or categories are hardcoded; all such values are derived from the Event returned by getEventDetails or from the EventRegistration returned by createEventRegistration.

components.functionality: Reads the id query parameter from the page URL, calls getEventDetails with that value, and exposes the loaded Event data or error state to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: On page initialization, read id from URL; if present, call getEventDetails({ eventId: id }); if missing, set a 'missingId' error state.
onUrlChange: If the id parameter changes (e.g., via navigation within the SPA), re-trigger getEventDetails with the new eventId.
onLoadSuccess: When getEventDetails returns successfully, store the event object in component state and clear any previous errors.
onLoadError: If getEventDetails fails or returns no event, set an 'eventLoadError' state that downstream components use to hide registration and show an error message.

components.functionality: Displays core event details retrieved via getEventDetails, including title, type, category, date/time, location, venue, description, pricing, and capacity indicators.
event_handlers: onEventDataChange: When the Event data from event-detail-loader updates, refresh all displayed fields and capacity status messages.
onUnavailableEvent: If Event.is_published is false or eventLoadError is set by the loader, hide this panel or show a high-level notice that the event is not available.

components.functionality: Shows the current registration capacity state for the event (e.g., seats remaining, registration closed) based on Event.capacity_total and Event.capacity_remaining.
event_handlers: onEventDataChange: When Event.capacity_remaining or Event.capacity_total changes in state, recompute whether registration is open, nearly full, or sold out and update status messages or disable the registration form via shared state.

components.functionality: Provides inputs for the user to select seats and a payment option, validates the input against the Event data, and submits a registration using createEventRegistration.
event_handlers: onInit: Initialize form state with default values (e.g., one seat, no payment option selected) when Event data becomes available and registration is allowed.

onChangeSeats: Update internal seats_reserved value when the user changes the seat count; validate against minimum (1) and Event.capacity_remaining when available; update computed total price using Event.price_per_seat.
onChangePaymentOption: When the user selects a payment option, ensure it is one of the values provided in Event.payment_options and store it in form state.

onValidate: Before submission, check that seats_reserved is valid (numeric and within capacity) and that a payment_option is selected and allowed for this Event; if any validation fails, prevent submission and show inline error messages.

onSubmit: If validation passes, call createEventRegistration({ eventId: Event.id, seats_reserved: seats_reserved_from_form, payment_option: payment_option_from_form }); set a 'submitting' state while waiting for the response.

onSubmitSuccess: When createEventRegistration returns success=true, store the returned registration object in state, update any total/summary info from registration.total_price and registration.currency when provided, optionally adjust displayed capacity_remaining locally if appropriate, and trigger the event-registration-confirmation component to display success.

onSubmitError: When createEventRegistration fails (network or success=false), capture registration or message from the response if present, show an error message to the user, keep the user’s inputs, and reset the 'submitting' state so the user can retry.

components.functionality: Displays a confirmation summary of the registration after a successful call to createEventRegistration, including seats reserved, payment option, and any pricing details returned by the API.
event_handlers: onRegistrationCreated: When event-registration-form receives a success=true response from createEventRegistration, display this component and populate it from registration; optionally hide or disable further edits in the registration form.

onDismissConfirmation: Allow the user to close or hide the confirmation panel while staying on the same event_detail.html page; this only updates local UI state and does not modify the EventRegistration on the server.

components.functionality: Provides contextual navigation actions from the event detail page to other pages, using the Event data to construct URLs with appropriate parameters.
event_handlers: onClickBackToCityEvents: When the user clicks 'Back to all events in this city', derive eventLocation from available Event location fields (for example, using Event.location_display without hardcoding any specific location) and navigate to events.html?location={eventLocation}.

onClickFindSimilarTalent: When the user clicks 'Find talent attending similar workshops', derive eventLocation as above and derive relatedCategory from Event.category or another appropriate event category field; navigate to talent_search.html?location={eventLocation}&primary_category={relatedCategory}.
onEventDataChange: Recompute the eventLocation and relatedCategory values when Event data changes, ensuring that navigation URLs always reflect the currently loaded Event.

components.functionality: Handles and displays errors related to missing URL parameters, failed event loading, or unavailable events, controlling the visibility of other components accordingly.
event_handlers: onMissingId: If id is not present in the URL, display an error message indicating that no event was specified, and hide the event-summary-panel and event-registration-form.

onEventLoadError: If getEventDetails fails or returns an invalid event, show an appropriate error message, hide registration-related components, and optionally provide a link back to events.html without a location filter.

onUnpublishedEvent: If Event.is_published is false, display a message that the event is not available for registration and disable or hide the registration form while still optionally showing read-only details.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a full-width container for the hero and a fixed max-width inner container for header content and navigation links.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a strict top-to-bottom, single-column flow inside a max-width central container, echoing the mockup’s linear structure: global site header (outside this scope), then a full-bleed event hero with the event summary centered, followed by stacked sections for registration and messaging. Structural components like navigation links sit just under the header and above the hero, while state-related elements (loader, errors, confirmation) overlay or replace the main content area without introducing sidebars or multi-column complexity.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a strict top-to-bottom, single-column flow inside a max-width central container, echoing the mockup’s linear structure: global site header (outside this scope), then a full-bleed event hero with the event summary centered, followed by stacked sections for registration and messaging. Structural components like navigation links sit just under the header and above the hero, while state-related elements (loader, errors, confirmation) overlay or replace the main content area without introducing sidebars or multi-column complexity.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve the top 10–15% of the viewport for the global header and event navigation links, then dedicate the majority of the first screen (roughly 60–70% of viewport height) to the full-width hero containing the event summary. Below the hero, allocate a generous vertical band (another 40–60% of viewport height depending on content) for the registration form or confirmation panel. Capacity status is compact and visually attached to the summary, using minimal space, while error and loader states temporarily occupy the central 50–60% of the viewport, replacing or overlaying content. Vertical gaps between major blocks follow the 48–64px rhythm, with inner padding of 16–24px per card.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-density, editorial feel: large typography for the event title, generous line-height, and substantial white (or image) space above and below key text blocks. Each functional block (summary, form, confirmation, error) is treated as a distinct, airy section with uncluttered layouts and minimal elements. Form fields are grouped with comfortable vertical spacing (16–24px) and ample padding inside the form card. Status indicators and navigation links are visually light and compact, never crowding the hero or the form.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Mimic the mockup’s flow: first a horizontal scan across the top—logo and main nav (global), then the event navigation links aligned within the content container. From there, the eye drops vertically into a dramatic, full-bleed hero image with a centrally positioned event title. The capacity status sits near the title, then the gaze naturally continues downward to the registration form as the main action area. Confirmation and error states take over the central vertical band when active, keeping attention centered. The teal brand color highlights key interactive and status elements (capacity pill, primary form button, confirmation accents), reinforcing the vertical narrative from top navigation to hero to action.
layout_strategies.overall_layout_description: Below the fixed site header, the page opens with a slim, understated row of event navigation links inside a centered, max-width container. Immediately beneath, a full-bleed, grayscale event image spans the entire browser width, forming an elegant hero. Over this image, a vertically centered inner container presents the event title in a large, serif headline, flanked or accompanied by a compact capacity status indicator. There is abundant space above and below the title, creating an editorial, magazine-like focal point. As you scroll slightly, a clean white registration card emerges below the hero, centered and spanning roughly two-thirds of the page width, with the form fields arranged in a calm, vertical rhythm. When errors occur or registration completes, the form area is replaced by a similarly sized, centered status panel, preserving the same visual hierarchy. Throughout, components are stacked in a single column, breathing with generous white space and tied together by a restrained palette, with teal reserved for primary actions and critical states.

layout.overall_description: The Event Detail & Registration page inherits the mockup’s calm, editorial structure: a thin band of navigation just under the global header, then a dramatic, image-led hero where the event’s title and capacity status sit in the visual center, surrounded by generous negative space. Beneath this, a single, centered registration card becomes the functional core of the page, occupying a comfortable portion of the width and height without ever feeling cramped. Supporting states—loading overlays, errors, and confirmation—either float above or take over this central column, ensuring that the user’s attention always remains on a strong vertical axis running from top navigation, through the hero headline, down to the primary action and its outcome. The overall impression is of a high-end agency editorial spread adapted into a focused, conversion-oriented detail page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as a semi-transparent overlay that covers the entire event content region, starting just below the fixed global header and spanning full viewport width and height. It dims the hero image, event summary, form, and any other content beneath with a subtle dark or soft white veil, keeping the underlying editorial layout visible but de-emphasized. In the exact center of the viewport—both horizontally and vertically—sits a minimal, teal-accented spinner or progress indicator, possibly accompanied by a short status line set in small sans-serif type. There is ample empty space around the loader element itself, avoiding any additional chrome, so the effect feels light and unobtrusive while clearly signaling a loading state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay anchored to the viewport, appearing above all other components when active and preventing interaction with underlying content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event summary panel occupies the hero region immediately under the global header and event navigation links. A grayscale or softly blurred event-related background image spans 100% of the viewport width and roughly 60–70% of its height on desktop, creating a full-bleed hero. Centered within this hero is an inner content container constrained to about 70–80% of the page width (aligned with the site’s standard max-width). The event title is set in large, two-line serif type, horizontally centered and positioned in the vertical middle third of the hero, with generous top and bottom margins (approximately 64px or more above and below the text) so it feels like a headline in a fashion magazine spread. Supporting metadata (date, location, category) appears directly below the title in a smaller, lighter sans-serif line, also centered and widely letter-spaced to maintain airiness. The capacity status component is visually attached to this panel, either as a pill aligned to the right edge of the inner container at the same vertical level as the metadata or tucked just below the title on the right, ensuring it feels like part of the hero rather than a separate block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow; the hero section remains positioned under the header and scrolls away naturally as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event capacity status is a compact pill-shaped indicator integrated into the event summary hero. It sits within the same inner container as the title—aligned to the right side of the container and vertically positioned either in line with the secondary metadata or just beneath the main headline, depending on the title length. The pill occupies only a small fraction of the width (around 10–15%), with horizontal padding in the 16–24px range and vertical padding of about 8px, echoing the navigation button styling from the mockup. It uses the teal brand color as a background or border to draw attention, with light, uppercase sans-serif text (e.g., ‘FEW SEATS LEFT’, ‘SOLD OUT’). There is clear breathing room around the pill (at least 16–24px from the title and metadata), so it feels like a precise editorial label rather than a crowded badge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and bound to the event summary panel; its position shifts responsively within that panel but does not scroll independently or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration form sits directly beneath the hero, separated from the event summary by a generous 48–64px vertical gap that allows the hero to feel distinct. The form is housed in a clean, white card centered on the page, spanning approximately 60–70% of the page width on desktop and never touching the viewport edges. Internally, the card carries 24px of padding on all sides, with larger 32px top padding to create a calm entry into the content. The form title (e.g., ‘Register for This Event’) is left-aligned within the card but visually centered within the overall width, set in a medium-weight serif or sans-serif that contrasts with the hero headline. Form fields are arranged in a single column, each spanning nearly the full width of the card with 16–24px vertical spacing between fields.
