- Behavior on navigation:
  - When the user activates a print control, the print-view-controller may update the URL to set mode to a print-specific value. This ensures the current state can be bookmarked or shared.
  - When the user navigates back or manually removes mode from the URL, the URL-state-controller reverts to the default viewing mode.

3. Combination of parameters
- The page supports combinations such as terms.html?section={sectionId}&mode={modeValue}. In this case:
  - The terms-loader always calls getTermsContent first.
  - After rendering, the section-link-handler scrolls to sectionId if present.
  - The print-view-controller applies print-specific behavior if mode indicates a print view.

4. Default behavior when parameters are absent
- If no section parameter is present, the page displays the top of the terms content with no automatic scrolling.
- If no mode parameter is present, the page uses the default reading view.

5. No additional persistence
- The page does not store state in localStorage or other client-side storage; state is fully derived from the URL parameters and the current result of getTermsContent. Any refresh of terms.html will re-fetch the content and reapply behavior based solely on the current URL.

components.functionality: Initializes the Terms & Conditions page by calling the getTermsContent interface, managing loading, success, and error states, and exposing the retrieved last_updated and content values to other components.

Renders the Terms & Conditions content returned by getTermsContent, displays the last-updated information, and exposes the rendered document structure (e.g., headings and anchors) to navigation-related components.

Inspects the rendered terms content (e.g., heading elements) and builds an interactive table of contents. Each entry corresponds to a section id present in the content, enabling users to click and navigate directly to that section.

Coordinates navigation to specific sections of the terms content based on user actions and URL parameters. On page load and when URL parameters change, it scrolls to the section indicated by the section parameter (if it matches an element id). When users click table-of-contents entries or in-content links, it scrolls to the target section and updates the section parameter in the URL.

Parses and manages URL query parameters relevant to the terms page (such as section and mode). It informs other components about the current parameters and listens for browser history changes to keep the internal state and visible content synchronized with the URL.

Handles print-focused behavior for the Terms & Conditions page. When mode indicates a print view or when a user activates a print control, it adjusts layout-related state (e.g., toggling optional UI elements) and may trigger the browser's print dialog once the terms content is ready.
Displays a non-intrusive error state if getTermsContent fails to load, and offers a user-triggered retry control that instructs the terms-loader to call getTermsContent again.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a full-width container for the hero and a fixed max-width inner container for header content and navigation links.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom arrangement as the mockup: a fixed global header (logo + navigation) at the very top, a full-bleed hero band directly beneath it, and then the main content area. For this Terms & Conditions page, the hero is simplified to a single-column content block centered over a grayscale, slightly blurred background image, with a large serif page title. Beneath the hero, the Terms layout shifts into a two-column editorial body: a left-side table of contents and a right-side main terms viewer, all within a centered max-width container. Utility elements like print controls and data/error states tie into this main content band rather than introducing new visual sections.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom arrangement as the mockup: a fixed global header (logo + navigation) at the very top, a full-bleed hero band directly beneath it, and then the main content area. For this Terms & Conditions page, the hero is simplified to a single-column content block centered over a grayscale, slightly blurred background image, with a large serif page title. Beneath the hero, the Terms layout shifts into a two-column editorial body: a left-side table of contents and a right-side main terms viewer, all within a centered max-width container. Utility elements like print controls and data/error states tie into this main content band rather than introducing new visual sections.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header occupies a shallow band of about 10–15% of the viewport height. The hero below it takes roughly 45–60% of the initial viewport height, with generous negative space above and below the title. The main content container below the hero uses a max-width (around 1120–1200px) centered in the viewport. Within this container on desktop, about 25–30% of the width on the left is allocated to the Terms TOC and about 55–60% on the right to the Terms content viewer, with the remaining width acting as outer margins. Vertical spacing between the hero and the main terms content is generous (48–64px), echoing the editorial spacing from the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density is kept low and airy. The hero shows only the page title (and optionally a subtle meta line like ‘Last updated’) with no buttons or extra UI clutter. In the content area, paragraphs use comfortable line height and left-aligned text in a limited column width to preserve readability. The TOC is a clean vertical list with ample line spacing, no heavy borders, and a single emphasis color (teal) for active items. Utility controls like the print action are compact, lightly styled, and placed with ample breathing room, avoiding visual noise around the main legal copy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup: the eye first catches the brand header at the top, then drops into the hero where the large ‘Terms & Conditions’ title sits centered over the full-bleed grayscale image. From that central hero focal point, the eye moves downward into the main content band. Within the content band, the flow shifts slightly horizontal: the TOC on the left acts as a subtle entry index, but the primary focus is the right-hand terms viewer. Active section highlighting and smooth scrolling keep attention moving vertically down the terms, with the teal accent used sparingly to mark active TOC items and key utilities (like the print control) without competing with the primary text content.

layout.overall_description: Below the fixed global header, a full-width hero fills the top of the Terms & Conditions page: a grayscale, softly blurred backstage or runway photograph stretches edge-to-edge, with a centered, large serif ‘Terms & Conditions’ title floating in the middle of the screen, surrounded by generous empty space top and bottom. The page feels calm and editorial, with no buttons distracting from the statement. As you scroll past the hero, the layout tightens into a refined reading environment inside a centered, max-width content container. On the left, a slim vertical column lists the sections of the terms in a clean table of contents, its entries lightly spaced and using teal only to highlight the current section. To the right, a wider column presents the actual legal text in a comfortable measure, with clear section headings and ample vertical spacing between articles. Above the main text, a small, understated utility row in the top-right corner offers a print action. Dynamic behaviors—loading states, deep-linking, smooth scrolling, and error feedback—are layered invisibly into this structure, ensuring that when data is loading a subtle overlay appears over the content area, and if something goes wrong, a centered, minimal error message card replaces the text while still respecting the overall spacious, monochrome aesthetic. The result feels like a high-end magazine’s legal backmatter: quiet, structured, and visually aligned with the brand’s fashion-forward hero design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-loader operates within the vertical band that starts just below the hero and spans down through the entire main content area. Visually, when active, it manifests as a subtle overlay over the terms-content-viewer region (and, optionally, the TOC region). On desktop, the overlay is constrained to the max-width content container centered on the page, not full-bleed: it spans roughly 80–90% of the viewport width, matching the main content’s width, with soft edges that stop before the outer page margins. A small, minimalist spinner or thin teal progress line is centered horizontally within the right-hand content column, approximately 1/3 of the way down from the top of the visible content area, leaving generous negative space around it. Beneath the spinner, optional skeleton blocks (light gray rectangles) may mimic the shape of headings and paragraphs inside the main content column, preserving the editorial column structure while signaling loading. The TOC column can either dim slightly or remain static; if dimmed, it uses a low-opacity white or gray wash that keeps the structure visible but de-emphasized. On mobile, the loader covers the full-width stacked content column (TOC above, content below) as a simple, centered spinner with ample margins above and below, maintaining the same calm, uncluttered feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The loader is non-visible when idle. When active, it overlays the content region as a semi-transparent layer positioned within the bounds of the main content container, not fixed to the viewport. It scrolls with the page content; if the user is mid-scroll, the loader remains anchored over the same content area rather than locking the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-content-viewer is the primary reading column located below the hero, within the central max-width container. On desktop, after a 48–64px vertical gap below the bottom of the hero, the content container begins. Inside a 12-column grid, the content viewer occupies the right, wider portion: roughly 7–8 of 12 columns, or about 55–65% of the container width, starting slightly right of center. The left margin of the content column aligns vertically with the right edge of the TOC, leaving a clear vertical gutter between them of about 24–32px. The text is left-aligned with a comfortable line length of roughly 60–80 characters, and top padding of 24–32px from the container’s top edge before the first heading, keeping the area airy. Section headings are stacked vertically with 24–32px spacing above them and 16–24px below, and paragraphs have 12–16px spacing between them, following the base 8px grid. The entire column floats in a large white field, with substantial white space on the right side of the container (roughly 1–2 columns) to maintain the editorial feel. On mobile and narrow viewports, this content viewer spans 100% of the width, stacking directly under a condensed or collapsed TOC, with 24px horizontal padding on both sides to preserve the feeling of an inset, readable column. The top of the content viewer includes a subtle metadata line (e.g., ‘Last updated’) aligned to the left and a print control aligned to the right, sharing the same horizontal row but leaving plenty of padding above and below (16–24px).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element within a centered max-width container. It scrolls vertically with the page and forms the main scrollable content area. On desktop, it aligns to a grid-based layout with a secondary column (TOC) on its left; on mobile, it reflows to a single-column layout beneath the TOC.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-toc appears as a slim, vertical navigation column anchored to the left side of the main content container, beginning just below the hero. On desktop, it occupies approximately 3 of 12 grid columns, or about 25–30% of the container width, and sits to the immediate left of the terms-content-viewer with a clear gutter between them. The top of the TOC aligns vertically with the top of the content viewer’s utility row, sitting about 48–64px below the hero’s bottom edge, reinforcing the sense that the hero is a separate, prominent band. Inside this column, TOC entries are rendered as a simple, left-aligned list with no heavy borders—each item occupies a horizontal band of about 24–32px height, with 8–12px of vertical space between items. The active section uses the brand teal color either as text color or a slim 2–3px left border line, while inactive items remain in neutral dark gray. The TOC title (e.g., ‘On this page’) appears at the top in small caps or light sans serif, spaced 16–24px below the top edge of the TOC column. Overall, the TOC feels like a discreet index rather than a bold sidebar, with generous white space around and inside. On mobile, the TOC reflows into a horizontal, full-width band that sits directly below the hero and above the content viewer. It may collapse into an accordion or horizontally scrollable pill list: entries arranged in a single row, left-aligned, with 8px vertical and 16px horizontal padding per pill, and a subtle underline or teal highlight for the active item. This mobile TOC band maintains 16–24px vertical padding top and bottom to keep it from feeling cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, the TOC column is sticky relative to the viewport: once the user scrolls past its initial top offset, it remains fixed within the left side of the content area, staying visible as the main text scrolls. It respects a top offset equal to the header plus a small margin (e.g., 80–96px from the viewport top), so it never overlaps the global header. On mobile, it behaves as a normal block element in the flow, scrolling away with the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-link-handler has no visible UI, but it governs the spatial relationship between the TOC and the content viewer. Conceptually, it occupies the same vertical band as the TOC and the terms-content-viewer combined. Each TOC entry is linked to a corresponding anchor in the content viewer, and activating a TOC entry triggers a smooth vertical scroll within the main content area, aligning the targeted section heading to a comfortable position slightly below the top of the viewport (taking into account the fixed header and any hero overlap). The scrolling behavior respects the editorial spacing: sections never snap hard against the viewport’s very top; instead, there is a 32–48px buffer above the heading after scroll. The handler also manages inline section links within the text, ensuring that jumping between sections preserves this same visual alignment and spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller with no explicit footprint in the layout. It intercepts clicks on TOC items and in-text section links to perform smooth, eased scrolling and section focus. It can also trigger transient focus styles (e.g., a brief background highlight) on the target heading within the content viewer without altering the overall layout structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-controller is invisible but tied to the entire page canvas. It ensures that the browser’s address bar reflects the currently viewed or active section of the terms via hash fragments (e.g., “#bookings”), and that loading the page with such a hash deep-links the user to the correct section. Spatially, it influences where the scroll position lands when a URL with a section hash is loaded: the targeted section heading is positioned comfortably below the top of the viewport, preserving a 32–48px visual buffer beneath the fixed header. It also updates the styling in the TOC—ensuring that the item corresponding to the visible section is highlighted in teal—so the TOC and content appear synchronized without introducing any additional visual elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, page-level controller. It listens to scroll events and URL hash changes, updating the active state of TOC items and triggering smooth scroll on initial load or when hashes change. It does not introduce fixed or floating UI; instead, it manipulates state that affects existing components’ visual states.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The print-view-controller exposes a single, subtle control integrated into the top region of the terms-content-viewer. On desktop, this appears as a small ‘Print’ text button or icon+label, right-aligned within the top utility row of the content column. The utility row sits 48–64px beneath the hero’s bottom edge and spans the width of the content viewer. The ‘Print’ action is positioned at the far right of this row, with approximately 16–24px padding from the content column’s right edge, and vertically centered in a band about 32–40px high. The label uses an understated sans-serif in small caps or all caps, with letterspacing to fit the editorial aesthetic, and may use the teal brand color for the text or a thin underline, but avoids heavy buttons or filled shapes. To the left of it, there may be metadata like ‘Last updated’ aligned to the left edge of the content column, with a comfortable spacing gap (24–32px) separating text from the print control. On mobile, this control remains in the same relative position—within the first 1–2 lines of the content area—but spans less horizontally; it may be right-aligned within the full-width content column, with at least 16–20px margins to the edges to maintain cleanliness.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the content column—part of the normal document flow, not fixed or sticky. When activated, it triggers a print-specific layout (e.g., hiding navigation, hero image, and TOC) but these changes are only reflected in the print media query, preserving the on-screen layout. The controller does not alter positioning except when a print dialog is invoked; the on-screen layout remains editorial and uncluttered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-error-state replaces the main content area visually when data fails to load. It is centered within the same max-width container that normally holds the TOC and content viewer, beginning in the same vertical band 48–64px below the hero. On desktop, rather than showing two columns, the layout collapses into a single, centered error card. This card occupies roughly 50–60% of the container width and is horizontally centered, leaving wide white margins on both sides to preserve the airy, editorial look. Vertically, the card is given generous padding—24–32px on all sides—with its content (an icon, a concise error headline, and a short explanation) center-aligned. The icon (if used) is small and line-based, possibly outlined in teal, and sits above the headline with 16px spacing. The headline uses a mid-sized serif or sans serif, not competing with the hero in scale, and there is 12–16px spacing before the descriptive text. A possible subtle action (e.g., ‘Try again’) is placed below, styled as a plain text link in teal rather than a heavy button, keeping the error appearance calm and unobtrusive. The TOC column is hidden in this state, so the user’s focus is entirely on the centered message. On mobile, the card spans nearly the full width with 16–24px side padding and is placed about 64px below the hero, maintaining comforting white space above and below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally, taking over the space normally occupied by the TOC and content viewer. It behaves as a normal block element within the scrolling page, not fixed or floating. While active, it suppresses or hides the TOC and content viewer components visually so that the error message is the sole focal point in the content area.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Displays the current privacy policy, including how data is collected, used, and protected, with search and section navigation powered by dynamic content from the privacy API.

core_features: Fetch and display the current privacy policy text and metadata using the getPrivacyPolicyContent interface.
Show the policy's last updated date so users know which version is currently in effect.
Support deep-link navigation to specific policy sections via URL parameters (e.g., ?section=...); automatically scroll and highlight the targeted section within the content.

Provide an in-page search function to find terms (e.g., related to profiles, bookings, castings, messages, analytics, and subscriptions) within the loaded policy content, driven by a URL query parameter (e.g., ?q=...).
Enable context-aware behavior using URL parameters (e.g., ?context=booking or ?context=casting) to guide users to the most relevant parts of the policy without changing the underlying content.

Offer a return navigation control (using URL parameters such as ?return_url=...) so users can easily go back to the page where they opened the policy (e.g., a booking request or event registration screen).
Provide a print/save control and a print mode (e.g., ?mode=print) that optimizes the policy for printing or exporting after the content has been loaded from the API.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Open and read the privacy policy from the footer
1. User clicks the 'Privacy Policy' link in the footer, which navigates to privacy.html with no additional parameters.
2. On page load, the privacy-policy-loader component calls getPrivacyPolicyContent.
3. While the request is in progress, the loader displays a loading state.
4. Once the API responds, the loader passes the last_updated and content values to the viewer and metadata components.
5. The privacy-policy-metadata-bar displays the last_updated value and confirms that the content shown is the current policy.
6. The privacy-policy-viewer renders the content in a scrollable area without any pre-selected section or highlights (because no URL parameters are set).
7. The user scrolls through the content; the policy-section-navigation updates the currently active section highlight as the user scrolls.

Workflow 2: Deep-link to a specific section (e.g., cookies or analytics)
1. From another page (for example, a cookie consent dialog), a link sends the user to privacy.html with a section parameter, such as privacy.html?section=cookies.
2. On page load, privacy-policy-loader calls getPrivacyPolicyContent.
3. After the content is loaded, the privacy-policy-viewer inspects the URL, reads section=cookies, and attempts to locate a matching anchor or heading inside the content.
4. The viewer scrolls to the corresponding section and visually emphasizes it (e.g., by temporary highlight or focus) without altering the content returned by the API.
5. The policy-section-navigation component reads the same section parameter and marks that section as active in the navigation list.
6. If the user clicks another section in the navigation, the section URL parameter is updated, and the viewer scrolls accordingly, all without additional API calls.

Workflow 3: Search within the privacy policy
1. A user arrives on privacy.html either directly or via a link that may already include a q parameter (e.g., privacy.html?q=booking).
2. privacy-policy-loader calls getPrivacyPolicyContent and provides content to the viewer.
3. policy-search-form reads the q parameter from the URL and pre-fills the search input, if present.
4. The privacy-policy-viewer receives the same q value and highlights all occurrences of that term in the rendered content.
5. If the user types a new search term and submits the form, policy-search-form updates the q parameter in the URL (using history APIs) and triggers a re-processing of the already loaded content for highlighting.
6. No additional calls to getPrivacyPolicyContent are made when the search term changes; only the local rendering and highlighting logic is updated.

Workflow 4: Contextual access from another feature (e.g., during booking or casting)
1. While creating a booking request or casting call on another page, the user clicks a 'Privacy Policy' link that opens privacy.html with context and return parameters, for example: privacy.html?context=booking&return_url=booking.html%3Fid%3D123.
2. privacy-policy-loader calls getPrivacyPolicyContent and passes the result to other components as in previous workflows.
3. The policy-context-banner (part of the metadata area) reads context=booking and maps it to the most relevant policy section (e.g., an anchor that explains how booking-related data is processed) without modifying any API data.
4. Once content is rendered, the viewer automatically scrolls to the identified section and may apply a temporary highlight.
5. The policy-context-return-link reads return_url and displays a clickable control that takes the user back to booking.html?id=123 when clicked.
6. The user reviews the relevant policy section and then uses the back control to return to the booking page.

Workflow 5: Print or save the policy
1. The user navigates to privacy.html either directly or with mode=print set (e.g., from a 'Print Privacy Policy' link: privacy.html?mode=print).
2. privacy-policy-loader calls getPrivacyPolicyContent.
3. After the content is successfully loaded and rendered, the policy-print-control checks the mode parameter.
4. If mode=print is detected, the component triggers the browser's print dialog once, allowing the user to print or save the policy as a PDF.
5. If the user is not in print mode, they can manually click a 'Print / Save' control, which triggers the same print dialog without changing the policy content or making extra API calls.
interactions: Click on footer 'Privacy Policy' link: Navigates to privacy.html, optionally with URL parameters for section, context, q, mode, and return_url.
Automatic data load on page initialization: The privacy-policy-loader component calls getPrivacyPolicyContent once when the page is initialized or refreshed.

Section navigation click: The user clicks a section name in policy-section-navigation; this updates the section parameter in the URL and instructs the viewer to scroll to the corresponding anchor within the currently loaded content.

In-page search submit: The user enters a term into policy-search-form and submits; the form updates the q parameter in the URL and triggers the viewer to highlight matching text in the existing content.
Clear search: The user clicks a 'Clear search' action in policy-search-form; this removes the q parameter from the URL and instructs the viewer to remove any active highlighting.

Contextual return click: The user clicks a back/return control (policy-context-return-link) that is built from return_url or related URL parameters; the browser navigates back to the originating page without modifying any API data.

Print action: The user clicks the print control in policy-print-control or arrives with mode=print; the component invokes the browser's print dialog after confirming that the policy content has been loaded.
Retry after error: If getPrivacyPolicyContent fails, the loader displays an error state with a retry control; the user clicks 'Retry', and the loader invokes getPrivacyPolicyContent again.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Privacy Policy page relies on URL parameters to manage view state while always loading the same current policy content via getPrivacyPolicyContent (which takes no input parameters). No state is stored in localStorage or other persistent client storage. The primary URL parameters and their effects are:

- section: A string identifier used to reference a specific section within the policy (e.g., 'cookies', 'analytics', 'messages'). After getPrivacyPolicyContent succeeds and the content is rendered, the privacy-policy-viewer scans for a matching anchor or heading and scrolls the view to that section. The policy-section-navigation component reads the same parameter to highlight the active section. Changing section updates the scroll position but does not trigger a new API call.

- q: A string representing an in-page search query. policy-search-form reads q on initialization to pre-fill the input. privacy-policy-viewer uses q to highlight matching text occurrences within the content. When the user submits a search, policy-search-form updates the q parameter (using history.pushState or history.replaceState) and notifies the viewer to re-run its search across the already loaded content. Removing q (e.g., via a clear button) removes the highlighting.

- context: A hint about the functional context from which the user opened the policy (e.g., 'talent_profile', 'booking', 'casting', 'subscription', 'event', 'contact', 'messages'). context is interpreted entirely on the client to map to one or more recommended sections (using a predefined mapping between contexts and section anchors). After getPrivacyPolicyContent resolves, the viewer and/or metadata components may use this mapping to auto-scroll to the relevant section and display a brief contextual note, but they do not modify the content returned by the API or make additional API calls.

- return_url (and optional auxiliary params like return_label): A URL-encoded string pointing back to the page where the user came from (e.g., booking.html%3Fid%3D123). policy-context-return-link reads return_url to render a back navigation control. Clicking this control causes a client-side navigation to the decoded URL. This parameter affects navigation only and does not influence any API interaction.

- mode: A view mode parameter that can be set to values such as 'print'. When mode=print is present, policy-print-control waits for getPrivacyPolicyContent to complete and for content to be rendered, then programmatically triggers the browser print dialog. Mode can also be used to apply minor layout adjustments suitable for printing. Changing mode never causes additional calls to getPrivacyPolicyContent.

State synchronization rules:
- On initial load, the page reads all current URL parameters and initializes components accordingly (loader state, search query, target section, context mapping, print mode, and return navigation).
- When the user interacts with controls that logically change state (such as selecting a different section or submitting a new search term), the relevant component updates the URL parameters using the History API without reloading the page. The updated URL then acts as the single source of truth for state, and components listening for URL changes (e.g., via a centralized state manager) react in a coordinated way.
- getPrivacyPolicyContent is called once per page load or explicit user-triggered refresh. All subsequent behavior (section changes, searches, mode toggles) is implemented on top of the already loaded content using the URL parameters as state, ensuring that no static or duplicated policy data is hardcoded in the page logic.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by calling getPrivacyPolicyContent, managing loading and error states, and distributing the resulting last_updated and content values to dependent components (metadata bar, viewer, section navigation, print control). Ensures that the policy data is fetched once per page load unless the user explicitly retries.

Displays high-level information about the loaded privacy policy, such as the last_updated timestamp, and optionally a brief contextual note derived from the context URL parameter. It also can expose a 'Copy link' action that copies the current URL (including section and q parameters) for sharing a deep link to the policy.

Renders the policy content string returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent as HTML or another supported markup. After initial render and whenever URL parameters change, it handles: 1) scrolling to a target section based on the section parameter; 2) highlighting search matches based on the q parameter; 3) applying temporary emphasis to context-recommended sections when context is present. It never modifies the underlying content; it only wraps or focuses parts of the rendered output.

Builds an in-page table of contents by analyzing the structure of the loaded content (e.g., extracting top-level headings) and exposing them as clickable items. When a user clicks a section item, it updates the section parameter in the URL and notifies the viewer to scroll. It also listens for changes to the section parameter to keep the active state in sync while the user scrolls manually.

Provides a search input for users to find specific terms within the currently loaded policy content. On initialization, it reads the q URL parameter to pre-populate the search field. On submit, it updates the q parameter in the URL (without reloading the page), which triggers the viewer to re-run highlighting logic over the existing content. It also supports clearing the search, which removes q from the URL and signals the viewer to remove highlighting.

Reads return_url (and optionally return_label) from the URL parameters and, when present, renders a return/back control at the top or bottom of the page. When the user clicks this control, the browser navigates to the decoded return_url. This component allows other parts of the site (e.g., booking forms, events, subscriptions) to send users to the privacy policy and then return them to the originating context, without requiring any additional API calls.

Manages printing behavior for the privacy policy. After confirming that privacy policy content has been loaded, it either: 1) automatically triggers the browser print dialog when mode=print is present in the URL; or 2) shows a user-invokable 'Print / Save' action that triggers the print dialog when clicked. It ensures printing is only activated after the content from getPrivacyPolicyContent is available so that the printed document contains the complete policy.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a full-width container for the hero and a fixed max-width inner container for header content and navigation links.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow: a shallow, full-width metadata bar directly beneath the site header, followed by a single-column, hero-like band that centers the search form. Below this, the layout transitions into a clean two-column content area where the left column carries section navigation and the right column presents the policy content viewer. Contextual controls (return link and print) live in the metadata bar at the top, mirroring the header/navigation pattern from the mockup. The data loader sits above all of this as a subtle overlay when active.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow: a shallow, full-width metadata bar directly beneath the site header, followed by a single-column, hero-like band that centers the search form. Below this, the layout transitions into a clean two-column content area where the left column carries section navigation and the right column presents the policy content viewer. Contextual controls (return link and print) live in the metadata bar at the top, mirroring the header/navigation pattern from the mockup. The data loader sits above all of this as a subtle overlay when active.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The metadata bar occupies a thin horizontal strip (about 10–15% of the initial viewport height) with generous left/right padding but modest height, echoing the shallow header feel. The search band beneath it uses a taller vertical slice (roughly 20–30% of the initial viewport height), with the search form centered and surrounded by ample negative space, functioning as a subdued utility hero. The main two-column area below fills the remaining page height: about 25–30% width for section navigation on the left and 70–75% for the policy viewer on the right, all constrained within a max-width content container. Vertical gaps between these bands follow the 48–64px spacing rhythm to maintain a calm, editorial feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays low and airy: each horizontal band (metadata bar, search band, content area) has clear breathing room above and below, with internal padding in the 16–24px range. The metadata bar displays only essential policy metadata and two key controls, avoiding clutter. The search area contains just the search form, centered in a large field of whitespace. In the main content, the section navigation is a short vertical list with clear separation between items, and the viewer uses generous line spacing and margins to keep long-form legal text readable without feeling heavy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visually, the eye first lands at the top-left on the context return link and policy title within the metadata bar, then scans right toward the teal-accented print control, echoing the mockup’s left-to-right header scan. From there, the layout naturally leads the eye downward into the centered search form, which acts as the focal point of the upper viewport. Once the user begins reading, attention shifts down and slightly left to the section navigation, then over to the main text in the viewer on the right, creating a gentle L-shaped scan. When the loader is active, it temporarily dominates the visual flow with a centered indicator over a subtly muted background, then recedes to restore the normal top-to-bottom reading path.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the site’s fixed global header, the Privacy Policy page opens with a slim, full-width metadata bar that feels like a refined sub-header: within a max-width centered container, a discreet return link anchors the top-left, policy metadata sits just right of center, and a teal-accented print button aligns on the far right. Below this, a spacious horizontal band uses a soft, light background tone to subtly separate it from the rest of the page; in its center, a wide search field spans about 60% of the page width, surrounded by generous top and bottom margins that echo the hero treatment from the mockup but in a utilitarian, text-first manner. As the eye moves downward, the layout resolves into a clean two-column grid: on the left, a narrow vertical strip provides section navigation that starts just under the search band and, on desktop, stays visible as you scroll; on the right, a broader column presents the policy text in a calm, editorial style, with ample white space around the paragraphs to maintain readability. All elements sit on a 12-column grid with consistent 16–24px interior padding and 48–64px vertical spacing between bands, keeping the page orderly and airy. When content is loading or updating, a translucent overlay gently dims everything from the metadata bar downward, with a centered loader indicator, briefly becoming the main focal point before fading out and returning focus to the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as a full-bleed overlay covering the entire privacy policy content area from the bottom edge of the global site header down to the footer. It is positioned fixed to the viewport, aligning with the page’s 12-column grid, and uses a semi-transparent white or very light gray scrim that subtly mutes the metadata bar, search band, section navigation, and content viewer beneath without fully obscuring them. At the exact center of the viewport, a minimal loader indicator (spinner or progress glyph) is displayed, occupying roughly 5–8% of the viewport width, with ample empty space around it to maintain the clean, premium feel. Horizontal and vertical centering of the loader reinforces the hero-like focus on this single element while active. Once loading finishes, the overlay fades away to restore normal interaction with the underlying layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a fixed overlay anchored to the viewport, spanning 100% width and height below the global header; visually and interactively sits above all other page components while active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The metadata bar is a slim horizontal band directly below the global site header, spanning the full browser width but with its content constrained to a centered max-width container (approximately 1120–1200px). Vertically, it occupies about 64–80px on desktop, with 16–24px of padding above and below the text, creating a shallow, refined strip. Inside the container, content aligns to a simple three-region structure: the left third holds the context return link (slightly inset from the left edge by 16–24px), the central area displays the policy title and metadata (such as last updated date) aligned slightly left-of-center, and the rightmost area reserves space for the print button aligned flush right. The background remains white or a very light neutral gray to match the editorial aesthetic, and the typography is set in a clean, slightly larger-than-body size serif or serif/sans pairing to echo the classic, magazine-like feel. The bar clearly separates itself above from the hero-like search band with a 32–48px gap below, reinforcing the page’s vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width bar positioned directly beneath the global header, scrolling away with the page content rather than remaining fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy viewer occupies the right-hand column of the main content area, starting just below the search band and aligned within the central max-width container. On desktop, it spans roughly 70–75% of the content width (about 8–9 columns of a 12-column grid), beginning with a 24–32px left margin from the section navigation column to maintain visual separation. The text area itself is framed with 24px padding on all sides, creating an airy reading canvas that keeps paragraph line lengths to an optimal 60–75 characters. Vertical spacing between headings and paragraphs follows the 16–24px increments, ensuring clear hierarchy and breathing room, while top and bottom margins of 48–64px separate the viewer from adjacent bands (search above, footer or subsequent sections below). The content scrolls naturally, with the viewer’s column extending downward beyond the initial viewport height as needed, while the left navigation remains visible for orientation. The overall impression is of an editorial article space: clean, typography-led, and free of unnecessary UI chrome.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scrollable column; its height grows with content and scrolls normally, while aligning to a right-side column in the desktop grid and stacking below navigation on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section navigation is positioned in a dedicated left-hand column directly beneath the search band, aligned to the same centered max-width container as the viewer. On desktop, it occupies roughly 25–30% of the content width (about 3–4 of 12 columns) and extends vertically alongside the policy viewer. The navigation items form a simple vertical list with each entry separated by 8–16px, leaving ample whitespace so the list feels light and scannable rather than dense. The left edge of this column aligns with the container’s left margin, with 16–24px padding inside the column to keep text away from the edge. A 32–48px vertical gap above the first navigation item visually separates it from the search band above, while a consistent 48–64px bottom margin keeps it from crowding any footer or end-of-page elements. On large screens, the navigation remains visible as you scroll the main content, creating a stable left anchor for orientation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, behaves as a left-side column that can be made sticky within the viewport (starting just below the search band) so the list stays in view while the viewer scrolls; on smaller screens, it stacks above the viewer and scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form sits in a distinct, hero-like band immediately below the metadata bar, centered horizontally within the page. This band spans the full browser width but keeps its contents within the central max-width container, with the search field itself taking up roughly 60% of the container width on desktop. Generous vertical spacing—about 48–64px above and below the field—creates a calm, open area that visually echoes the mockup’s hero but replaces imagery with a simple, functional focus on search. The search input is horizontally centered in the container, with 8–12px of internal vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal padding inside the field, giving it a substantial, editorial presence. The band may use a very light, neutral background tint to subtly differentiate it from the pure white of the metadata bar and main content area, reinforcing its role as a functional focal point in the upper portion of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, single-column element centered within its own horizontal band; scrolls away naturally as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The context return link is placed in the far-left portion of the metadata bar, acting as the first element a user encounters when scanning from left to right. It aligns with the left edge of the central max-width container, with a 16–24px inset from the container boundary to keep it from hugging the screen edge. Vertically, it is centered within the metadata bar’s height, sitting on the same baseline or just above the policy title to suggest a hierarchical relationship—like a subtle breadcrumb or ‘back to overview’ control. The link uses understated typography (smaller than the page title but clearly legible) and may include a left-pointing chevron icon, echoing the logo’s anchor role from the mockup without competing visually with the policy title. Ample whitespace around it ensures it reads as a discrete, easy-to-tap/ click control rather than part of the body copy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Embedded within the metadata bar’s left region; static and scrolls away with the bar, with no sticky behavior on its own.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The print control is positioned on the far-right side of the metadata bar, visually balancing the context return link on the left and acting as the primary accent control in this band. It aligns with the right edge of the central max-width container, inset by 16–24px, and is vertically centered within the bar’s height. The button itself uses solid teal as its background color, with white text, echoing the standout ‘FRANÇAIS’ button in the mockup and drawing the eye to the upper-right corner during the initial left-to-right scan. It is sized with comfortable padding—about 8px vertically and 16–24px horizontally—so it feels substantial yet refined, without overwhelming the adjacent policy metadata text. A small gap of 16–24px separates it from any metadata text to its left, preserving clarity and the minimal, editorial aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Embedded in the right region of the metadata bar; static and scrolls away with the bar, with no fixed or sticky behavior.
